Key Takeaway: Slicing returns a VIEW — modifying it modifies the original. Always use .copy() when you want independence. Boolean indexing is faster than loops for filtering large arrays.

blogs.title: NumPy Mathematics: Vectorized Ops, Statistics, and Linear Algebra
blogs.description: A complete guide to NumPy's mathematical capabilities — element-wise operations, trigonometric functions, broadcasting rules, statistics, and linear algebra with np.linalg.
blogs.author: NumPy Notes
blogs.content:
Element-Wise Mathematics

NumPy operations apply to every element without writing a loop. This is called vectorization.

a = np.array([2, 4, 6])

a + 2   # [4, 6, 8]
a * 2   # [4, 8, 12]
a ** 2  # [4, 16, 36]
a / 2   # [1., 2., 3.]

b = np.array([1, 2, 3])
a + b   # Element-wise: [3, 6, 9]

Trigonometric and Math Functions

np.sin(a)
np.cos(a)
np.sqrt(a)
np.log(a)     # Natural log
np.exp(a)     # e^x

Broadcasting — The Power Feature

Broadcasting lets NumPy perform operations on arrays of different shapes without copying data. The rules work from the trailing (rightmost) dimensions.

a = np.array([1, 2, 3])       # Shape: (3,)
b = np.array([[10], [20]])    # Shape: (2, 1)

a + b
# Result shape: (2, 3)
# [[11, 12, 13],
#  [21, 22, 23]]

Broadcasting Rules: Two dimensions are compatible if they are equal, or one of them is 1. If one is 1, it gets stretched to match.

Statistics

mat = np.random.randint(1, 100, size=(4, 6))

np.min(mat)           # Global minimum
np.max(mat, axis=0)   # Column-wise maximum
np.min(mat, axis=1)   # Row-wise minimum
np.sum(mat, axis=0)   # Column-wise sum

np.mean(mat)
np.median(mat)
np.std(mat)           # Standard deviation
np.var(mat)           # Variance
np.argmax(mat)        # Index of maximum value
np.argmin(mat, axis=1) # Row-wise index of minimum

Linear Algebra

from numpy import linalg

a = np.ones((2, 3))
b = np.full((3, 4), 4)

# Matrix multiplication (shapes must be compatible)
np.matmul(a, b)  # (2x3) @ (3x4) = (2x4)
a @ b            # Same, cleaner syntax

# Determinant (square matrices only)
np.linalg.det(np.identity(3))

# Other linalg functions
np.linalg.inv(A)          # Matrix inverse
np.linalg.solve(A, b)     # Solve Ax = b
np.linalg.eig(A)          # Eigenvalues and eigenvectors
np.linalg.norm(A)         # Matrix norm
np.linalg.matrix_rank(A)  # Rank

Handling NaN and Infinity

a = np.array([1.0, np.nan, 3.0, np.inf])

np.isnan(a)      # [False, True, False, False]
np.isinf(a)      # [False, False, False, True]
np.nanmean(a)    # Mean ignoring NaN
np.nansum(a)     # Sum ignoring NaN
np.nan_to_num(a, nan=0.0, posinf=1e9)

Key Takeaway: Broadcasting is one of NumPy's most powerful features — understanding the rules saves you from many reshape operations. Use nanmean/nansum to safely aggregate arrays with missing values.

blogs.title: NumPy Advanced: np.where, Boolean Ops, Sorting, and Searching
blogs.description: Master NumPy's filtering and searching toolkit — np.where for vectorized conditionals, np.select for multi-condition logic, np.take, sorting with argsort, and useful utility functions.
blogs.author: NumPy Notes
blogs.content:
np.where — Vectorized IF-ELSE

np.where is the NumPy equivalent of an if-else statement, applied element-wise across an entire array.

arr = np.array([10, 5, 30, 3])

# Mode 1: Select values based on condition
np.where(arr > 10, "High", "Low")
# ['Low', 'Low', 'High', 'Low']

# Mode 2: Get indices where condition is True
np.where(arr > 10)  # (array([2]),) — index 2 satisfies

# Mode 3: Clamp values
arr2 = np.where(arr < 10, arr, 10)  # Cap at 10
# [10, 5, 10, 3]

np.any() and np.all()

arr = np.array([0, 0, 5, 0])
np.any(arr > 0)     # True — at least one is > 0

arr2 = np.array([2, 4, 6])
np.all(arr2 % 2 == 0)  # True — all are even

# Axis-wise
mat = np.array([[0, 0, 1], [2, 3, 4]])
np.any(mat > 0, axis=1)  # [True, True]
np.all(mat > 0, axis=1)  # [False, True]

np.select — Multiple Conditions

arr = np.array([5, 12, 25, 40, 7])

conditions = [arr < 10, (arr >= 10) & (arr < 30), arr >= 30]
choices = ["Low", "Medium", "High"]

np.select(conditions, choices, default="Unknown")
# ['Low', 'Medium', 'Medium', 'High', 'Low']

np.take — Fast Index Selection

arr = np.array([10, 20, 30, 40])
np.take(arr, [1, 3])  # [20, 40]

# Works on 2D with axis
np.take(mat, [0, 2], axis=1)  # Columns 0 and 2

Sorting

a = np.array([3, 1, 4, 1, 5])
np.sort(a)        # Returns sorted copy
a.sort()          # In-place sort

# argsort — get indices that would sort the array
np.argsort(a)     # Very useful for custom ordering
a[np.argsort(a)]  # Same as sorted

# Sort 2D rows by a column
order = np.argsort(mat[:, 0])  # Sort by first column
mat[order]

Other Useful Functions

np.clip(arr, 0, 100)           # Limit values to range
np.unique(arr, return_counts=True)  # Unique + frequencies
np.argmax(arr)                 # Index of maximum
np.nonzero(arr)                # Indices of non-zero elements
np.argwhere(arr > 10)          # Coordinates where True

Key Takeaway: np.where is the vectorized replacement for Python if-else loops. np.select handles multiple conditions like an if-elif-else chain. Use argsort when you need the ordering, not just the sorted values.

blogs.title: Pandas Series and DataFrame: The Foundation of Data Analysis
blogs.description: A complete introduction to Pandas — what Series and DataFrame are, how they are created, their key properties, and the conceptual difference between Pandas and NumPy.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
What is Pandas?

Pandas is a Python library built on NumPy that provides two primary data structures for tabular data analysis: Series (1D) and DataFrame (2D). It is the standard tool for data loading, cleaning, transformation, and exploration in Python.

Series — Labeled 1D Array

A Series is a 1-dimensional labeled array that can hold any data type. Think of it as a single column from a spreadsheet.

import pandas as pd

# Creating Series
s = pd.Series([10, 20, 30])                    # Auto index
s = pd.Series([10, 20, 30], index=['a','b','c'])  # Custom index
s = pd.Series({'a': 10, 'b': 20})              # From dict

# Properties
s.values    # Underlying NumPy array
s.index     # Index labels
s.dtype     # Data type
s.name = "marks"

DataFrame — Labeled 2D Table

A DataFrame is a 2D labeled data structure with rows and columns. Each column is a Series. Think of it as an Excel sheet, a SQL table, or a dict of Series.

# From dict (most common)
data = {"name": ["Alice", "Bob"], "age": [25, 30]}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# From list of dicts (API/JSON pattern)
data = [{"a": 1, "b": 2}, {"a": 3, "b": 4}]
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# From NumPy array
df = pd.DataFrame(np.ones((3, 3)), columns=['a','b','c'])

# From zip
names = ['Tom', 'Jane']
ages = [25, 30]
df = pd.DataFrame(list(zip(names, ages)), columns=["name", "age"])

DataFrame Properties

df.shape      # (rows, cols)
df.ndim       # 2
df.dtypes     # Data type per column
df.columns    # Column labels
df.index      # Row labels
df.info()     # Full summary
df.describe() # Statistical summary of numeric columns

Series vs DataFrame

FeatureSeriesDataFrame
Dimensions1D2D
StructureSingle columnTable
Data typesOne dtypeMultiple dtypes
IndexRow index onlyRow + column labels

Key Takeaway: df["col"] returns a Series. df[["col"]] returns a DataFrame. This distinction matters when you chain operations.

blogs.title: Pandas Data Selection: loc, iloc, Boolean Filtering, and query()
blogs.description: Master all methods to select data from a Pandas DataFrame — column selection, loc for label-based access, iloc for positional access, boolean masks, and the powerful query() method.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
Column Selection

# Single column — returns Series
df['Name']

# Multiple columns — returns DataFrame
df[['Name', 'Age']]

# Columns by position range
df[df.columns[1:4]]

loc — Label-Based Selection

loc uses row/column labels. The end index is inclusive.

df.loc[0, 'Name']         # Single value
df.loc[0:2, 'A':'C']      # Row 0-2, columns A-C (inclusive)
df.loc[[0,1,2], ['A','C']] # Specific rows and columns

# Condition + select columns (best practice)
df.loc[df['Age'] > 25, ['Name', 'Age']]

iloc — Position-Based Selection

iloc uses integer positions. The end index is exclusive (like Python slicing).

df.iloc[0]           # First row
df.iloc[[4,5,6]]     # Rows 4, 5, 6
df.iloc[0, 1]        # Row 0, column 1
df.iloc[0:3, 1:3]    # Slice by position

Boolean Indexing — The Right Way

Pandas filtering is based on boolean masks — a Series of True/False values.

# Single condition
df[df['Salary'] >= 50000]

# Multiple conditions — use & | ~ NOT and/or/not
df[(df['Age'] < 40) & (df['Salary'] > 50000)]
df[(df['Age'] < 25) | (df['Dept'] == 'IT')]
df[~(df['Dept'] == 'IT')]  # NOT

# Always use parentheses around each condition!

Useful Filtering Methods

df[df['City'].isin(['NY', 'LA'])]    # In a list
df[df['Age'].between(25, 40)]        # Range
df[df['Name'].str.startswith('A')]   # String pattern
df[df['Salary'].notna()]             # Non-null values

query() — SQL-Like Filtering

df.query("Age < 40 & Salary > 50000 & Dept == 'IT'")

# Using Python variables with @
min_salary = 50000
df.query("Salary > @min_salary")

# IN operator
df.query("Dept in ['IT', 'HR']")

Copy vs View Warning

# Wrong — may cause SettingWithCopyWarning
filtered = df[df['Age'] > 25]
filtered['Age'] = 0  # Warning!

# Correct
filtered = df.loc[df['Age'] > 25].copy()
filtered['Age'] = 0  # Safe

Key Takeaway: Use loc for label-based access, iloc for positional. Always use & | ~ for multiple conditions, never Python's and/or/not. Always .copy() when creating a filtered subset you plan to modify.

blogs.title: Handling Missing Data in Pandas: Detection, Filling, and Dropping
blogs.description: A complete guide to missing data in Pandas — detecting NaN values, filling with constants or statistics, forward/backward fill, interpolation, and when to drop rows or columns.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
What is Missing Data?

Pandas represents missing data as NaN (Not a Number) for numeric types, None for object types, and NaT for datetime types. Missing data occurs from data entry errors, sensor failures, optional fields, and data integration issues.

Detecting Missing Values

df.isnull()            # True where missing
df.isna()              # Same as isnull
df.notnull()           # True where present

df.isnull().sum()      # Count per column
df.isnull().mean() * 100  # % missing per column

# Filter rows with missing values
df[df['Gender'].isnull()]
df[df['Gender'].notnull()]

Filling Missing Values

# Fill all with zero
df.fillna(0)

# Column-wise with statistics (best practice)
df['Age'].fillna(df['Age'].mean(), inplace=True)
df['Salary'].fillna(df['Salary'].median(), inplace=True)
df['Category'].fillna(df['Category'].mode()[0], inplace=True)

# Column-wise with dict
df.fillna({'salary': df['salary'].mean(), 'age': 0})

# Forward fill (use previous value)
df.ffill()

# Backward fill (use next value)
df.bfill()

# With limit (only fill N consecutive NaNs)
df.ffill(limit=2)

replace() vs fillna()

# replace() is for any value, not just NaN
df.replace(np.nan, -999)      # Sentinel value
df.replace("N/A", np.nan)     # Clean string placeholders

Interpolation — Smart Filling for Time Series

df.interpolate(method='linear')                  # Basic linear
df.interpolate(method='linear', limit_direction='both')  # Fill both directions

Use interpolation for sensor data, stock prices, and other time series — never for categorical data or IDs.

Dropping Missing Values

df.dropna()                    # Drop rows with any NaN
df.dropna(how='all')           # Drop rows where ALL values are NaN
df.dropna(axis=1)              # Drop columns with NaN
df.dropna(thresh=3)            # Keep rows with at least 3 non-null values
df.dropna(subset=['Salary'])   # Drop only if Salary is NaN

Common Mistakes

  • Comparing with NaN directly: df[df['Age'] == np.nan] — always returns empty. Use isnull().
  • Using ffill/bfill on salary or financial columns without business justification
  • Dropping rows without first checking what percentage is missing

Key Takeaway: Always inspect missing data with isnull().sum() before deciding strategy. Fill with statistics for numeric columns, use mode for categoricals. Only drop if the percentage is small or all values in a row are missing.

blogs.title: Pandas groupby: Split-Apply-Combine Explained
blogs.description: Master Pandas groupby — the split-apply-combine pattern, aggregation with agg(), transformation with transform(), group filtering, and all key parameters explained.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
The Mental Model: Split → Apply → Combine

Every groupby operation follows this pattern: Split the DataFrame into groups, Apply a function to each group, and Combine the results. This replaces manual loops and filters with a single expression.

groupby() Syntax and Parameters

df.groupby(
    by=None,           # Column(s) to group by
    as_index=True,     # Group keys as index (False = as columns)
    sort=True,         # Sort group keys
    dropna=True        # Exclude NaN groups
)

Basic Grouping

# Group by single column
gender_groups = df.groupby('Gender')

# Iterate over groups
for name, group in gender_groups:
    print(name, group['Salary'].count())

# Group by multiple columns
team_gender = df.groupby(['Gender', 'Team'])

Aggregation with agg()

# Single aggregation
df.groupby('Team')['Salary'].mean()

# Multiple aggregations on same column
df.groupby('Team').agg(
    min_salary=('Salary', 'min'),
    max_salary=('Salary', 'max'),
    avg_salary=('Salary', 'mean'),
    emp_count=('Team', 'count')
)

# Multiple columns, multiple functions
df.groupby('Dept').agg({
    'Salary': ['mean', 'max'],
    'Age': 'median'
})

transform() — Same-Shape Output

Unlike aggregation which reduces rows, transform() returns results aligned to the original DataFrame — essential for adding group-level statistics back as columns.

# Rank each employee within their team
df['Rank_in_Team'] = df.groupby('Team')['Salary'].transform(
    lambda x: x.rank(ascending=False)
)

# Z-score within group
df['salary_zscore'] = df.groupby('Team')['Salary'].transform(
    lambda x: (x - x.mean()) / x.std()
)

# Fill NaN with group mean
df['Salary'] = df.groupby('Team')['Salary'].transform(
    lambda x: x.fillna(x.mean())
)

filter() — Keep or Drop Entire Groups

# Keep only teams where mean salary >= 1M
high_pay_teams = df.groupby('Team').filter(
    lambda x: x['Salary'].mean() >= 1_000_000
)

size() vs count()

df.groupby('Team').size()           # Total rows per group (includes NaN)
df.groupby('Team')['Age'].count()   # Non-null values per group

Key Takeaway: Use agg() for summaries, transform() when you need results at the original row level, filter() to include/exclude whole groups. Use as_index=False for cleaner output when exporting.

blogs.title: Pandas Combining DataFrames: concat, merge, and join
blogs.description: When to use concat vs merge vs join in Pandas — understanding the mental model, all merge types (inner, left, right, outer), and the correct approach for each real-world scenario.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
The Decision Model

MethodUse WhenSQL Equivalent
concat()Same structure, stack or alignUNION
merge()Match rows by column valuesJOIN
join()Match rows by indexIndex-based JOIN

pd.concat() — Stacking Data

pd.concat(
    objs,               # List of DataFrames
    axis=0,             # 0=stack rows, 1=stack columns
    join='outer',       # 'outer'=union, 'inner'=intersection
    ignore_index=False, # Reset index after concat
    keys=None           # Add hierarchical index
)

# Vertical stacking
result = pd.concat([df1, df2], ignore_index=True)

# Horizontal stacking
pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=1)

# Track source
pd.concat([df1, df2], keys=['train', 'test'])

pd.merge() — SQL-Style Joins

pd.merge(
    left, right,
    how='inner',       # 'inner','left','right','outer'
    on=None,           # Column(s) in both DataFrames
    left_on=None,      # Column in left (when names differ)
    right_on=None,     # Column in right
    suffixes=('_x','_y')  # Handle overlapping column names
)

# Simple inner join
pd.merge(orders, customers, on='customer_id')

# Left join (keep all left rows)
pd.merge(orders, customers, how='left', on='id')

# Merge on different column names
pd.merge(df1, df2,
         left_on='acc_number',
         right_on='account_NO',
         how='inner')

# Multi-key merge
pd.merge(df1, df2, on=['date', 'store_id'])

Understanding join types

  • inner: Only matching keys — intersection
  • left: All left rows, NaN for unmatched right
  • right: All right rows, NaN for unmatched left
  • outer: All rows from both, NaN where no match

DataFrame.join() — Index-Based

# Default: joins on index
df1.join(df2, how='left', lsuffix='_l', rsuffix='_r')

# Join using column from left to index of right
df1.join(df2, on='key', how='inner')

Common Mistakes

  • Using concat when data has different schemas — check column names first
  • Forgetting suffixes when column names overlap
  • Using join() with multiple key columns — use merge() instead
  • Not checking for duplicate keys before merge (can cause row explosion)

Key Takeaway: concat for stacking same-structure tables, merge for relational joins on column values (covers 90% of join use cases), join for index-based alignment. Always check key uniqueness before merging.

blogs.title: Pandas pivot() and pivot_table(): Reshaping Data for Analysis
blogs.description: Understand when and how to use Pandas pivot and pivot_table — the difference between them, all parameters explained, multiple aggregation functions, and real-world use cases.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
Why Pivoting Exists

Real data is usually in long format (one row per observation). Analysts often need it in wide format (one row per entity, different conditions as columns). Pivoting transforms between these forms.

pivot() — Pure Reshape, No Aggregation

df.pivot(
    index='Category',      # What becomes rows
    columns='Product',     # What becomes columns
    values='Amount'        # What fills the cells
)

Critical rule: pivot() fails if any index-column combination appears more than once. It cannot aggregate — it only reshapes. In real-world data, duplicates almost always exist, so pivot() is rarely used in production.

pivot_table() — Reshape + Aggregate

df.pivot_table(
    index=['Product', 'Category'],  # Row keys
    columns='Quantity',             # What becomes columns
    values='Amount',                # Values to aggregate
    aggfunc='mean',                 # Aggregation function
    fill_value=0,                   # Replace NaN in output
    margins=False                   # Add row/column totals
)

# Multiple aggregations
df.pivot_table(
    index='Category',
    values='Amount',
    aggfunc=['mean', 'median', 'min', 'count']
)

pivot vs pivot_table

Featurepivotpivot_table
Handles duplicates❌ No✅ Yes
Aggregation❌ No✅ Yes
Production useRareVery common

Practical Example

sales = pd.DataFrame({
    'Product': ['Apple', 'Apple', 'Banana', 'Banana'],
    'Region': ['North', 'South', 'North', 'South'],
    'Revenue': [100, 150, 80, 120]
})

# Total revenue per Product per Region
pivot = sales.pivot_table(
    index='Product',
    columns='Region',
    values='Revenue',
    aggfunc='sum',
    fill_value=0
)

Key Takeaway: Think of pivot_table as groupby + aggregation + reshape combined. Use it instead of pivot in real data — it handles duplicates, supports multiple aggregations, and can add totals via margins=True.

blogs.title: Pandas Sorting, String Operations, and Data Type Conversion
blogs.description: Master Pandas sort_values() and sort_index() with all parameters, the .str accessor for vectorized string operations, and safe data type conversion with astype() and pd.to_datetime().
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
Sorting with sort_values()

df.sort_values(
    by='Age',            # Column or list of columns
    ascending=True,      # True=asc, False=desc, list=[True,False]
    inplace=False,       # Modify original
    na_position='last',  # 'first' puts NaN at top
    ignore_index=False,  # Reset index after sort
    kind='mergesort'     # Stable sort for equal elements
)

# Single column
df.sort_values('Salary', ascending=False)

# Multiple columns
df.sort_values(['Dept', 'Salary'], ascending=[True, False])

# Case-insensitive sort with key
df.sort_values('Name', key=lambda col: col.str.lower())

Sorting with sort_index()

df.sort_index()              # Sort rows by index label
df.sort_index(ascending=False)
df.sort_index(axis=1)        # Sort columns alphabetically

String Operations — The .str Accessor

The .str accessor enables vectorized string operations on a Series. It handles NaN automatically and is far faster than using .apply() with a Python function.

df['Name'].str.lower()
df['Name'].str.upper()
df['Name'].str.strip()       # Remove whitespace
df['Name'].str.len()         # Length of each string
df['Name'].str.replace('Elena', 'Emily')
df['Name'].str.contains('an', case=False, na=False)  # Returns boolean
df['Name'].str.startswith('E')
df['Name'].str.split('a')    # Returns list per row
df['Name'].str.split('a', expand=True)  # Returns DataFrame
df['Name'].str.count('a')    # Count occurrences
df['Name'].str.findall(r'[A-Z]')  # Regex match all
df['Name'].str.isnumeric()   # Check numeric string
df['Name'].str.cat(sep=', ') # Concatenate all values

Data Type Conversion

# astype() — direct conversion
df['Age'] = df['Age'].astype(int)
df['Salary'] = df['Salary'].astype(float)
df = df.astype({'Age': 'float64', 'Name': 'string'})

# errors='ignore' — leave column unchanged if conversion fails
df.astype({'A': int}, errors='ignore')

# pd.to_numeric() — safe for messy numbers
pd.to_numeric(df['Salary'], errors='coerce')  # Invalid → NaN

# pd.to_datetime() — best for date parsing
df['JoinDate'] = pd.to_datetime(df['JoinDate'], errors='coerce')
df['JoinDate'] = pd.to_datetime(df['JoinDate'], format='%d/%m/%Y')

# After converting to datetime, use .dt accessor
df['JoinDate'].dt.year
df['JoinDate'].dt.month
df['JoinDate'].dt.weekday

category Type — Memory Optimization

df['Gender'] = df['Gender'].astype('category')
# Saves memory and speeds up groupby on low-cardinality columns

Key Takeaway: Always use pd.to_datetime() for dates (not astype), and pd.to_numeric() with errors='coerce' for messy numeric columns. Use .str accessor instead of .apply(str.method) for vectorized performance.

blogs.title: Pandas I/O: Reading and Writing CSV and JSON Files
blogs.description: Complete guide to Pandas file I/O — all key parameters of read_csv() and to_csv(), handling JSON with read_json() and json_normalize() for nested data, and reading from URLs and APIs.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
pd.read_csv() — The Most Used Function

pd.read_csv(
    filepath_or_buffer,    # Path, URL, or file object
    sep=',',               # Delimiter
    header='infer',        # Row number for column names
    index_col=None,        # Column to use as index
    usecols=None,          # Load only specific columns
    nrows=None,            # Read only first N rows
    skiprows=None,         # Skip specific rows
    na_values=None,        # Additional strings to treat as NaN
    parse_dates=None,      # Columns to parse as datetime
    engine=None            # 'c' or 'python'
)

Key Parameters in Practice

# Load only needed columns (saves memory on large files)
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', usecols=['Name', 'Salary', 'Dept'])

# Set index at load time (more efficient than set_index later)
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', index_col='EmployeeID')

# Handle common missing value patterns
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', na_values=['N/A', 'NULL', 'none', ''])

# Parse dates during load
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', parse_dates=['JoinDate', 'BirthDate'])

# Handle different delimiters
df = pd.read_csv('data.tsv', sep='\t')
df = pd.read_csv('dirty.csv', sep='[,:_|]', engine='python')

# Preview large files
df = pd.read_csv('huge.csv', nrows=100)

# Skip metadata rows
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', skiprows=[0, 1, 3])

# Read from URL
df = pd.read_csv('https://example.com/data.csv')

df.to_csv() — Saving Data

import csv

df.to_csv('output.csv', index=False)               # No index column
df.to_csv('output.csv', index=False, header=False) # No header
df.to_csv('output.csv', sep='|')                   # Custom separator
df.to_csv('output.csv', na_rep='NA')               # Replace NaN
df.to_csv('output.csv', mode='a', header=False)    # Append mode
df.to_csv('output.csv', encoding='utf-8')
df.to_csv('output.csv', quoting=csv.QUOTE_ALL)     # Quote everything

JSON Handling

import json

# Read flat JSON
df = pd.read_json('data.json')

# Nested JSON — use json_normalize
with open('nested.json') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
df = pd.json_normalize(data)

# API responses
import requests
response = requests.get('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts')
df = pd.json_normalize(response.json())

# JSON Lines (one JSON per line — logs, streaming)
df = pd.read_json('events.jsonl', lines=True)

JSON Orient Options

df.to_json(orient='records')   # [{col:val}, ...] — best for APIs
df.to_json(orient='split')     # {columns, index, data} — round-trip
df.to_json(orient='index')     # {row_label: {col:val}}
df.to_json('file.json', orient='table')  # With schema metadata

Key Takeaway: Always specify usecols and parse_dates when reading large CSV files. Use json_normalize for nested JSON — pd.read_json() alone cannot handle nested structures. When appending to CSV, always pass header=False.

blogs.title: Pandas Data Cleaning: Duplicates, Normalization, and Iteration
blogs.description: Complete data cleaning workflows in Pandas — detecting and removing duplicate rows, normalizing numeric features with Min-Max and MaxAbs scaling, and choosing the right iteration method.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
Handling Duplicate Rows

Detecting Duplicates

# Returns True for rows that are duplicates
df.duplicated()                      # All columns
df.duplicated(subset=['Name'])       # Specific columns
df.duplicated(keep='last')           # Mark first as duplicate
df.duplicated(keep=False)            # Mark ALL occurrences

df.duplicated().sum()                # Count duplicates
df[df.duplicated()]                  # View duplicate rows

Removing Duplicates

df.drop_duplicates()                 # Remove exact duplicates
df.drop_duplicates(subset=['Name', 'Age'])  # By subset
df.drop_duplicates(keep='last')      # Keep last occurrence
df.drop_duplicates(keep=False)       # Remove all occurrences
df.drop_duplicates(ignore_index=True)  # Reset index after

Real-World Considerations

# Case sensitivity matters
df['Name'] = df['Name'].str.lower()

# Whitespace matters
df['Name'] = df['Name'].str.strip()

df.drop_duplicates(subset=['Name'])

Data Normalization

Normalization rescales numeric features so no single column dominates because of larger magnitude — essential before ML modeling or multi-feature comparison.

Max Absolute Scaling — Range [-1, 1]

max_scaled = df.copy()
for col in max_scaled.columns:
    max_scaled[col] = max_scaled[col] / max_scaled[col].abs().max()

Best when you want to preserve sparsity and signs (positive/negative) matter.

Min-Max Scaling — Range [0, 1]

min_max_scaled = df.copy()
for col in min_max_scaled.columns:
    min_max_scaled[col] = (
        min_max_scaled[col] - min_max_scaled[col].min()
    ) / (min_max_scaled[col].max() - min_max_scaled[col].min())

The most common normalization method, used in ML, neural networks, and distance-based algorithms. Sensitive to outliers.

Iteration — Choosing the Right Method

Pandas is built for vectorized operations, not loops. Iteration should be a last resort.

MethodSpeedUse When
Vectorized opsFastestMath, filtering, transformations
apply(axis=1)FastRow-wise custom logic
itertuples()MediumRow iteration when needed
iterrows()SlowLearning / debugging only
# Avoid this
result = []
for i, row in df.iterrows():
    result.append(row['A'] + row['B'])

# Do this instead
df['C'] = df['A'] + df['B']

# When you need row logic — use apply
df['Label'] = df.apply(
    lambda row: 'High' if row['Salary'] > 100000 else 'Low',
    axis=1
)

# When you must loop — use itertuples (fastest loop)
for row in df.itertuples():
    print(row.Name, row.Salary)

Key Takeaway: Clean before deduplication — strip whitespace and standardize case first. Always normalize only feature columns, never the target variable. Prefer vectorization over any form of loop in Pandas.

blogs.title: Pandas Index Management: set_index, reset_index, and MultiIndex
blogs.description: Everything about Pandas indexes — why they exist, set_index and reset_index with all parameters, the difference between loc and iloc with custom indexes, and an introduction to MultiIndex.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
What is an Index?

The Pandas index is the row label system — it is the identity of each row. Without it, operations like .loc, merge, join, and align would not work. Think of it as the primary key for your DataFrame rows.

# Default RangeIndex created automatically
df = pd.DataFrame({'Name': ['A', 'B'], 'Age': [25, 30]})
print(df.index)  # RangeIndex(start=0, stop=2, step=1)

# Index properties
df.index.values      # NumPy array of labels
df.index.dtype       # Data type
df.index.is_unique   # Check for duplicates

set_index() — Make a Column the Index

df.set_index(
    keys,                  # Column name or list of names
    drop=True,             # Remove column after setting
    inplace=False,
    verify_integrity=False # Error on duplicate index values
)

# Basic example
df_indexed = df.set_index('EmployeeID')
df.loc['E001']   # Now can access by ID

# Keep column AND set as index
df.set_index('Name', drop=False)

# Prevent accidental duplicate indexes
df.set_index('Email', verify_integrity=True)

reset_index() — Move Index Back to Column

df.reset_index(
    drop=False,   # Keep old index as a column (default)
    inplace=False
)

df.reset_index()         # Old index becomes a column
df.reset_index(drop=True)  # Discard old index, fresh RangeIndex

Named Index

df.index.name = 'EmployeeID'  # Give index a meaningful name

Sorting by Index

df.sort_index()              # Alphabetical / numeric sort
df.sort_index(ascending=False)

MultiIndex — Hierarchical Indexes

# Created naturally by groupby
grouped = df.groupby(['Dept', 'Team'])['Salary'].mean()
# Index is now (Dept, Team) pairs

# Access MultiIndex
grouped.loc['Engineering']           # All Engineering rows
grouped.loc[('Engineering', 'Alpha')]  # Specific group

# Reset to flat DataFrame
grouped.reset_index()

Common Patterns

# Load CSV and set index immediately (efficient)
df = pd.read_csv('employees.csv', index_col='EmployeeID')

# After groupby + aggregation, reset to clean output
agg = df.groupby('Dept').agg(avg_salary=('Salary','mean'))
agg_clean = agg.reset_index()

Key Takeaway: Index is not just a row number — it is the alignment key for all Pandas operations. Use set_index when a column uniquely identifies rows. Use verify_integrity=True in data pipelines to catch data quality issues early.

blogs.title: Pandas End-to-End: A Complete Data Analysis Workflow
blogs.description: Put it all together — a complete real-world Pandas workflow covering data loading, exploration, cleaning, transformation, aggregation, and export with best practices at each step.
blogs.author: Pandas Notes
blogs.content:
Step 1: Load and First Look

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load data efficiently
df = pd.read_csv('employees.csv',
    usecols=['Name', 'Dept', 'Salary', 'JoinDate', 'Gender'],
    parse_dates=['JoinDate'],
    na_values=['N/A', 'none', '']
)

# First inspection
print(df.shape)          # Dimensions
df.info()                # Types + null counts
df.describe()            # Statistical summary
df.head()                # First 5 rows

Step 2: Understand Data Quality

# Missing value audit
missing = df.isnull().mean() * 100
print(missing[missing > 0].sort_values(ascending=False))

# Duplicates
print(f"Duplicates: {df.duplicated().sum()}")

# Data types
print(df.dtypes)

Step 3: Clean

# Standardize text
df['Name'] = df['Name'].str.strip().str.title()
df['Dept'] = df['Dept'].str.strip().str.upper()

# Fill missing values strategically
df['Salary'] = df['Salary'].fillna(df.groupby('Dept')['Salary'].transform('median'))
df['Gender'].fillna('Unknown', inplace=True)

# Remove duplicates
df = df.drop_duplicates(subset=['Name', 'Dept'], ignore_index=True)

# Fix data types
df['Salary'] = pd.to_numeric(df['Salary'], errors='coerce')

Step 4: Transform and Engineer Features

# Derived column
df['Years_Employed'] = (pd.Timestamp.now() - df['JoinDate']).dt.days // 365

# Rank within department
df['Salary_Rank'] = df.groupby('Dept')['Salary'].transform(
    lambda x: x.rank(ascending=False, method='min')
)

# Salary tier
df['Salary_Tier'] = pd.cut(
    df['Salary'],
    bins=[0, 40000, 80000, 150000, float('inf')],
    labels=['Entry', 'Mid', 'Senior', 'Executive']
)

Step 5: Aggregate and Summarize

# Department summary
dept_summary = df.groupby('Dept').agg(
    head_count=('Name', 'count'),
    avg_salary=('Salary', 'mean'),
    max_salary=('Salary', 'max'),
    min_years=('Years_Employed', 'min')
).round(2).reset_index()

# Gender distribution per department
gender_pivot = df.pivot_table(
    index='Dept',
    columns='Gender',
    values='Name',
    aggfunc='count',
    fill_value=0
)

Step 6: Filter and Export

# Select high-performing departments
high_pay = df.loc[
    df['Salary'] > df['Salary'].quantile(0.75),
    ['Name', 'Dept', 'Salary', 'Salary_Rank']
].copy()

# Export
dept_summary.to_csv('dept_summary.csv', index=False)
high_pay.to_excel('high_performers.xlsx', index=False)

Best Practices Summary

  • Always use usecols and parse_dates when loading large files
  • Audit missing values before any transformation
  • Prefer transform() over apply() for group-aligned operations
  • Use .copy() when creating subsets you will modify
  • Chain operations but use intermediate variables for debugging
  • Use category dtype for low-cardinality string columns
  • Always reset_index after groupby for clean exportable output

Key Takeaway: A good Pandas workflow is systematic — load, inspect, clean, transform, aggregate, export. Each step has specific tools designed for it. Master these tools and 90% of data analysis problems become straightforward.

blogs.title: Introduction to SQL & SQL Server: The Foundation of Data
blogs.description: Understand what SQL is, what a DBMS is, the types of databases, and why SQL Server is the go-to relational database for data professionals.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is SQL?

SQL stands for Structured Query Language. It is the standard language used to communicate with relational databases. SQL allows you to store, retrieve, update, and delete data in a structured and efficient way.

What is a DBMS?

A Database Management System (DBMS) is software that manages databases. It acts as an interface between the user and the database, handling how data is stored, organized, and retrieved. Examples include MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and Microsoft SQL Server.

What is SQL Server?

Microsoft SQL Server is a relational DBMS developed by Microsoft. It is widely used in enterprise environments for storing and querying structured data. SQL Server uses T-SQL (Transact-SQL), an extension of SQL with additional procedural programming features.

Types of Databases

  • Relational Databases (RDBMS) — Data is stored in tables with rows and columns. Relationships are defined using keys. (SQL Server, MySQL, PostgreSQL)
  • NoSQL Databases — Designed for unstructured or semi-structured data. Types include document stores, key-value, graph, and columnar. (MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis)
  • In-Memory Databases — Store data in RAM for ultra-fast access. (Redis, SAP HANA)
  • Cloud Databases — Hosted and managed on cloud platforms. (Azure SQL, Amazon RDS, Google BigQuery)

Database Structure

A relational database is organized in a hierarchy: Server → Database → Schema → Table → Columns & Rows. Each table stores data about one entity (e.g., Customers, Orders), and tables are related to each other via primary keys and foreign keys.

How Tables Are Stored

Internally, SQL Server stores table data in pages (8 KB blocks) and extents (8 pages = 64 KB). Data can be stored as row-store (traditional row-based format) or columnstore (column-based, ideal for analytics). Understanding this helps you design efficient indexes and queries.

Why Learn SQL?

  • SQL is the universal language for data — every data role uses it
  • Works across all major databases with minor syntax differences
  • Essential for data engineering, analytics, and backend development
  • Powers dashboards, reports, ETL pipelines, and data warehouses

One-Line Summary: SQL is the language of data, and SQL Server is Microsoft's powerful relational database engine — together they form the backbone of most enterprise data systems.

blogs.title: SQL Components & the SELECT Statement
blogs.description: Break down the anatomy of a SQL query — statements, clauses, keywords, functions, identifiers, operators, and values — and learn how the SELECT statement works.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
Anatomy of a SQL Query

Every SQL query is made up of distinct building blocks. Understanding them helps you read and write queries with confidence.

-- Retrieve Customers Data
SELECT name, LOWER(country)
FROM customers
WHERE country = 'Italy'

SQL Components Explained

  • SQL Statement — The complete instruction given to the database (the entire query above is one statement)
  • Comment — Text preceded by -- that is ignored by the engine but helps developers understand the code
  • Clauses — Major sections of a statement: SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, HAVING
  • Keywords — Reserved words with special meaning in SQL: SELECT, FROM, WHERE, AND, OR, NOT
  • Functions — Built-in operations applied to data: LOWER(), UPPER(), SUM(), COUNT()
  • Identifiers — Names of database objects: tables (customers), columns (name, country)
  • Operators — Symbols for comparisons and logic: =, >, <, AND, OR, LIKE
  • Values — Literal data in a query: 'Italy', 100, NULL

Types of SQL Commands

  • DQL (Data Query Language) — SELECT — Used to retrieve data
  • DDL (Data Definition Language) — CREATE, DROP, ALTER — Used to define structure
  • DML (Data Manipulation Language) — INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — Used to modify data

The SELECT Statement

The SELECT statement is the most commonly used SQL command. It retrieves data from one or more tables.

-- Select all columns
SELECT * FROM customers

-- Select specific columns
SELECT name, country FROM customers

-- Filter rows
SELECT name FROM customers WHERE country = 'Italy'

-- Sort results
SELECT name FROM customers ORDER BY name ASC

-- Limit rows (SQL Server)
SELECT TOP 10 name FROM customers

-- Remove duplicates
SELECT DISTINCT country FROM customers

SELECT Clause Order

SQL clauses must follow a specific order: SELECT → FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → ORDER BY

One-Line Summary: A SQL query is composed of structured components — clauses, keywords, functions, identifiers, and operators — and SELECT is the primary tool for retrieving data from a database.

blogs.title: Filtering Data in SQL: WHERE, Operators & Conditions
blogs.description: Learn how to precisely filter rows using WHERE clauses, comparison operators, logical operators, IN, BETWEEN, LIKE, and NULL checks.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
The WHERE Clause

The WHERE clause filters rows based on a condition. Only rows that satisfy the condition are returned.

SELECT * FROM orders WHERE total_amount > 500

Comparison Operators

  • = — Equal to
  • <> or != — Not equal to
  • > — Greater than
  • < — Less than
  • >= — Greater than or equal to
  • <= — Less than or equal to
SELECT * FROM products WHERE price >= 100 AND price <= 500

Logical Operators

  • AND — Both conditions must be true
  • OR — At least one condition must be true
  • NOT — Negates a condition
SELECT * FROM customers
WHERE country = 'Germany' AND age > 30

SELECT * FROM customers
WHERE country = 'Italy' OR country = 'Spain'

SELECT * FROM customers
WHERE NOT country = 'USA'

BETWEEN Operator

BETWEEN filters rows within an inclusive range.

SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'

IN and NOT IN Operators

Use IN to match a column against a list of values — much cleaner than multiple OR conditions.

SELECT * FROM customers
WHERE country IN ('Italy', 'Germany', 'France')

SELECT * FROM customers
WHERE country NOT IN ('USA', 'UK')

LIKE Operator (Pattern Matching)

LIKE is used to filter text data based on patterns using wildcards.

  • % — Matches any sequence of characters
  • _ — Matches any single character
-- Names starting with 'A'
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE name LIKE 'A%'

-- Names ending with 'son'
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE name LIKE '%son'

-- Names with exactly 4 characters
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE name LIKE '____'

NULL Checks

NULL means missing or unknown value. You cannot use = to check for NULL — use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL.

SELECT * FROM customers WHERE email IS NULL
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE phone IS NOT NULL

One-Line Summary: SQL's filtering tools — WHERE, comparison operators, IN, BETWEEN, LIKE, and NULL checks — give you precise control over which rows your query returns.

blogs.title: GROUP BY & HAVING: Aggregating and Filtering Groups
blogs.description: Master how GROUP BY aggregates data into groups and how HAVING filters those groups — a critical skill for summary reports and business analytics.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is GROUP BY?

GROUP BY collapses multiple rows into a single summary row per group. It is always used with aggregate functions like COUNT(), SUM(), AVG(), MAX(), and MIN().

-- Count customers per country
SELECT country, COUNT(*) AS total_customers
FROM customers
GROUP BY country

Common Aggregate Functions

  • COUNT(*) — Counts rows in each group
  • SUM(column) — Adds up values in a column
  • AVG(column) — Calculates the average
  • MAX(column) — Returns the highest value
  • MIN(column) — Returns the lowest value
SELECT category,
  COUNT(*) AS total_products,
  AVG(price) AS avg_price,
  MAX(price) AS max_price
FROM products
GROUP BY category

GROUP BY with Multiple Columns

You can group by more than one column to create finer-grained summaries.

SELECT country, city, COUNT(*) AS customers
FROM customers
GROUP BY country, city

The HAVING Clause

HAVING filters groups after aggregation — unlike WHERE which filters rows before aggregation. Use HAVING when your condition involves an aggregate function.

-- Only show countries with more than 100 customers
SELECT country, COUNT(*) AS total
FROM customers
GROUP BY country
HAVING COUNT(*) > 100

WHERE vs HAVING

  • WHERE — Filters individual rows before grouping. Cannot use aggregate functions.
  • HAVING — Filters grouped results after aggregation. Can use aggregate functions.
-- Correct: WHERE filters rows, HAVING filters groups
SELECT country, SUM(order_total) AS revenue
FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01'
GROUP BY country
HAVING SUM(order_total) > 10000

One-Line Summary: GROUP BY creates summary rows per group using aggregate functions, and HAVING lets you filter those groups — together they are the core of analytical SQL queries.

blogs.title: SQL JOINs: Combining Data from Multiple Tables
blogs.description: Learn all JOIN types — INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL OUTER, and CROSS JOIN — with real-world examples and visual explanations to master multi-table queries.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is a JOIN?

A JOIN combines rows from two or more tables based on a related column — usually a primary key and a foreign key. Instead of storing all data in one big table, relational databases spread data across multiple tables and JOIN them when needed.

INNER JOIN

Returns only rows that have matching values in both tables.

SELECT c.name, o.order_date, o.total
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id

Use when: you only want records that exist in both tables.

LEFT JOIN (LEFT OUTER JOIN)

Returns all rows from the left table, and matched rows from the right table. Unmatched rows from the right show as NULL.

SELECT c.name, o.order_date
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id

Use when: you want all customers, even those with no orders.

RIGHT JOIN (RIGHT OUTER JOIN)

Returns all rows from the right table, and matched rows from the left. Unmatched left rows show as NULL.

SELECT c.name, o.order_date
FROM customers c
RIGHT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id

FULL OUTER JOIN

Returns all rows from both tables. Where there's no match, NULLs are filled in from either side.

SELECT c.name, o.order_date
FROM customers c
FULL OUTER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id

CROSS JOIN

Returns the Cartesian product — every row from the left table combined with every row from the right. Use carefully as it can produce massive result sets.

SELECT c.name, p.product_name
FROM customers c
CROSS JOIN products p

Self JOIN

Joins a table to itself. Useful for hierarchical data like employees and managers.

SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.employee_id

JOIN Best Practices

  • Always use explicit ANSI-style joins (INNER JOIN, not old comma syntax)
  • Use table aliases to keep queries readable
  • Index the columns used in the ON clause for better performance
  • Filter before joining large tables when possible

One-Line Summary: JOINs are the heart of relational SQL — they let you combine data from multiple tables using shared keys, with different JOIN types controlling how unmatched rows are handled.

blogs.title: DDL Commands in SQL Server: CREATE, ALTER & DROP
blogs.description: Learn how to define and manage your database structure using Data Definition Language commands — CREATE, ALTER, DROP — and understand constraints like PRIMARY KEY and FOREIGN KEY.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is DDL?

Data Definition Language (DDL) is the set of SQL commands used to define and manage the structure of a database. Unlike DML (which changes data), DDL changes the schema — the tables, columns, constraints, and relationships.

CREATE TABLE

Used to create a new table with defined columns and data types.

CREATE TABLE customers (
  customer_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name NVARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  email NVARCHAR(150) UNIQUE,
  country NVARCHAR(50),
  created_date DATE DEFAULT GETDATE()
)

Common Data Types in SQL Server

  • INT — Integer numbers
  • BIGINT — Large integer numbers
  • DECIMAL(p,s) — Precise decimal numbers
  • NVARCHAR(n) — Variable-length Unicode text
  • DATE — Date without time
  • DATETIME — Date with time
  • BIT — Boolean (0 or 1)

Constraints

  • PRIMARY KEY — Uniquely identifies each row. Cannot be NULL.
  • FOREIGN KEY — Links to another table's primary key. Enforces referential integrity.
  • NOT NULL — Column must have a value
  • UNIQUE — All values in the column must be different
  • DEFAULT — Provides a default value when none is supplied
  • CHECK — Validates that values meet a condition
CREATE TABLE orders (
  order_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  customer_id INT FOREIGN KEY REFERENCES customers(customer_id),
  total DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (total >= 0),
  status NVARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'Pending'
)

ALTER TABLE

Used to modify an existing table — add columns, change data types, or add/drop constraints.

-- Add a new column
ALTER TABLE customers ADD phone NVARCHAR(20)

-- Modify a column's data type
ALTER TABLE customers ALTER COLUMN name NVARCHAR(200)

-- Drop a column
ALTER TABLE customers DROP COLUMN phone

DROP TABLE

Permanently deletes a table and all its data. Use with extreme caution.

DROP TABLE customers

-- Safer: check if it exists first
IF OBJECT_ID('customers', 'U') IS NOT NULL
  DROP TABLE customers

TRUNCATE vs DROP

  • TRUNCATE — Removes all rows but keeps the table structure
  • DROP — Removes the entire table including structure

One-Line Summary: DDL commands (CREATE, ALTER, DROP) let you define and maintain the structure of your database — tables, columns, data types, and constraints — forming the blueprint of your data.

blogs.title: DML Commands: INSERT, UPDATE & DELETE
blogs.description: Master the three Data Manipulation Language commands that control how data is added, changed, and removed from SQL Server tables.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is DML?

Data Manipulation Language (DML) contains the SQL commands that modify the actual data stored in tables — not the structure, but the records themselves.

INSERT — Adding Data

The INSERT statement adds new rows to a table.

-- Insert a single row
INSERT INTO customers (customer_id, name, country)
VALUES (1, 'Alice Johnson', 'Germany')

-- Insert multiple rows
INSERT INTO customers (customer_id, name, country)
VALUES
  (2, 'Marco Rossi', 'Italy'),
  (3, 'Sophie Dubois', 'France')

-- Insert from another table (SELECT INTO)
INSERT INTO customers_backup
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE country = 'Germany'

UPDATE — Modifying Data

The UPDATE statement modifies existing rows. Always use a WHERE clause to avoid updating every row in the table.

-- Update a single record
UPDATE customers
SET country = 'Austria'
WHERE customer_id = 1

-- Update multiple columns
UPDATE orders
SET status = 'Shipped', ship_date = GETDATE()
WHERE order_id = 1001

-- Update based on another table
UPDATE customers
SET country = s.new_country
FROM customers c
JOIN staging_updates s ON c.customer_id = s.customer_id

DELETE — Removing Data

The DELETE statement removes rows from a table. Always include a WHERE clause unless you intend to delete all rows.

-- Delete a specific row
DELETE FROM customers WHERE customer_id = 5

-- Delete rows matching a condition
DELETE FROM orders WHERE status = 'Cancelled'

-- Delete all rows (but keep table)
DELETE FROM temp_log

DELETE vs TRUNCATE

  • DELETE — Removes specific rows, can be rolled back, fires triggers, slower on large tables
  • TRUNCATE — Removes all rows instantly, faster, cannot be conditionally filtered, resets identity columns

Using Transactions with DML

Wrap DML operations in transactions to ensure data integrity. If something goes wrong, you can roll back.

BEGIN TRANSACTION
  UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 500 WHERE id = 1
  UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 500 WHERE id = 2
COMMIT TRANSACTION
-- Use ROLLBACK TRANSACTION if an error occurs

One-Line Summary: DML commands (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) are how you write data into SQL Server — always pair UPDATE and DELETE with a WHERE clause to avoid accidental mass changes.

blogs.title: SQL String Functions: Manipulating Text Data
blogs.description: A complete guide to SQL Server's built-in string functions — LOWER, UPPER, TRIM, LEN, CONCAT, SUBSTRING, LEFT, RIGHT, and REPLACE — with practical examples.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
Why String Functions Matter

Real-world data is messy. Names have extra spaces, emails have mixed casing, and product codes need parsing. SQL Server's built-in string functions let you clean, transform, and extract text data directly in your queries without needing external tools.

LOWER() and UPPER()

Convert text to lowercase or uppercase.

SELECT LOWER('Hello World')   -- 'hello world'
SELECT UPPER('hello world')   -- 'HELLO WORLD'

-- Standardize email for comparison
SELECT * FROM users WHERE LOWER(email) = 'alice@example.com'

TRIM(), LTRIM(), RTRIM()

Remove leading and/or trailing spaces from text.

SELECT TRIM('  hello  ')    -- 'hello'
SELECT LTRIM('  hello')    -- 'hello'
SELECT RTRIM('hello  ')    -- 'hello'

LEN()

Returns the number of characters in a string (excluding trailing spaces).

SELECT LEN('Hello')         -- 5
SELECT name, LEN(name) AS name_length FROM customers

CONCAT()

Combines multiple strings into one. Handles NULLs more gracefully than the + operator.

SELECT CONCAT(first_name, ' ', last_name) AS full_name
FROM employees

-- CONCAT_WS: join with a separator
SELECT CONCAT_WS(', ', city, state, country) AS address
FROM locations

SUBSTRING()

Extracts a portion of a string by position.

-- SUBSTRING(string, start_position, length)
SELECT SUBSTRING('Hello World', 1, 5)  -- 'Hello'
SELECT SUBSTRING(phone, 1, 3) AS area_code FROM customers

LEFT() and RIGHT()

Extract a specified number of characters from the left or right of a string.

SELECT LEFT('Hello World', 5)   -- 'Hello'
SELECT RIGHT('Hello World', 5)  -- 'World'

-- Get last 4 digits of a card number
SELECT RIGHT(card_number, 4) AS last_four FROM payments

REPLACE()

Replaces all occurrences of a substring with another string.

SELECT REPLACE('Hello World', 'World', 'SQL')  -- 'Hello SQL'

-- Remove dashes from phone numbers
SELECT REPLACE(phone, '-', '') FROM customers

CHARINDEX() and PATINDEX()

-- Find position of a character
SELECT CHARINDEX('@', email) AS at_position FROM users

-- Pattern match position
SELECT PATINDEX('%@%.com', email) FROM users

One-Line Summary: SQL Server's string functions — LOWER, UPPER, TRIM, LEN, CONCAT, SUBSTRING, LEFT, RIGHT, REPLACE — are essential tools for cleaning and transforming text data in your queries.

blogs.title: Date & Time Functions in SQL Server
blogs.description: Learn how to work with dates and times in SQL Server using GETDATE, DATEADD, DATEDIFF, DATEPART, FORMAT, and more — essential for time-based analytics.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
Why Date Functions Are Critical

Almost every business dataset involves dates — order dates, hire dates, transaction timestamps. SQL Server provides rich date/time functions to filter, extract, calculate, and format temporal data efficiently.

Getting the Current Date and Time

SELECT GETDATE()          -- Current date and time (datetime)
SELECT GETUTCDATE()       -- Current UTC date and time
SELECT SYSDATETIME()      -- Higher precision current datetime
SELECT CAST(GETDATE() AS DATE)  -- Today's date only

DATEADD()

Add or subtract a time interval from a date.

-- DATEADD(interval, number, date)
SELECT DATEADD(DAY, 7, GETDATE())    -- 7 days from now
SELECT DATEADD(MONTH, -3, GETDATE()) -- 3 months ago
SELECT DATEADD(YEAR, 1, '2024-01-01') -- 2025-01-01

DATEDIFF()

Calculate the difference between two dates.

-- DATEDIFF(interval, start_date, end_date)
SELECT DATEDIFF(DAY, '2024-01-01', '2024-12-31')   -- 365 days
SELECT DATEDIFF(MONTH, hire_date, GETDATE()) AS months_employed
FROM employees

DATEPART() and YEAR(), MONTH(), DAY()

Extract specific parts from a date.

SELECT DATEPART(YEAR, GETDATE())    -- 2025
SELECT DATEPART(MONTH, GETDATE())   -- 8
SELECT DATEPART(DAY, GETDATE())     -- current day
SELECT DATEPART(WEEKDAY, GETDATE()) -- day of week (1=Sunday)

-- Shorthand
SELECT YEAR(order_date), MONTH(order_date) FROM orders

FORMAT()

Format dates into custom string patterns.

SELECT FORMAT(GETDATE(), 'yyyy-MM-dd')      -- '2025-08-09'
SELECT FORMAT(GETDATE(), 'dd MMMM yyyy')   -- '09 August 2025'
SELECT FORMAT(GETDATE(), 'MMM dd, yyyy')   -- 'Aug 09, 2025'

CONVERT() and CAST() for Dates

SELECT CAST('2025-08-01' AS DATE)
SELECT CONVERT(DATE, '01/08/2025', 103)  -- British format dd/mm/yyyy

Practical Time-Based Filtering

-- Orders from this year
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE YEAR(order_date) = 2025

-- Orders from last 30 days
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= DATEADD(DAY, -30, GETDATE())

-- Monthly revenue report
SELECT YEAR(order_date) AS yr, MONTH(order_date) AS mo,
  SUM(total) AS revenue
FROM orders
GROUP BY YEAR(order_date), MONTH(order_date)
ORDER BY yr, mo

One-Line Summary: SQL Server's date functions — GETDATE, DATEADD, DATEDIFF, DATEPART, FORMAT — let you perform time-based calculations and extract insights from temporal data with precision.

blogs.title: NULL Functions in SQL Server: Handling Missing Data
blogs.description: Understand NULL in SQL — what it means, why it matters, and how to handle it gracefully using ISNULL, COALESCE, NULLIF, and CASE expressions.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is NULL?

In SQL, NULL represents the absence of a value — it means unknown or missing. NULL is not zero, not an empty string, and not false. It is a special marker that requires special handling.

NULL Behavior

NULL does not equal anything — not even itself. Any arithmetic or comparison with NULL results in NULL.

SELECT NULL = NULL     -- NULL (not TRUE!)
SELECT 5 + NULL       -- NULL
SELECT 'hello' + NULL -- NULL

-- Correct way to check for NULL
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE phone IS NULL
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE phone IS NOT NULL

ISNULL()

Replaces NULL with a specified replacement value.

-- ISNULL(expression, replacement)
SELECT ISNULL(phone, 'N/A') AS phone FROM customers
SELECT ISNULL(discount, 0) AS discount FROM orders

COALESCE()

Returns the first non-NULL value from a list of expressions. More flexible than ISNULL as it accepts multiple arguments and is ANSI-standard.

-- Returns first non-NULL value
SELECT COALESCE(mobile, home_phone, work_phone, 'No contact') AS contact
FROM customers

SELECT COALESCE(discount, 0) + price AS final_price FROM products

NULLIF()

Returns NULL if two expressions are equal; otherwise returns the first expression. Useful to avoid division-by-zero errors.

-- Returns NULL if value equals 0 (prevents divide-by-zero)
SELECT total / NULLIF(quantity, 0) AS unit_price FROM orders

SELECT NULLIF(status, 'Unknown') FROM tickets

NULL in Aggregations

Aggregate functions like COUNT(), SUM(), and AVG() ignore NULL values. This can sometimes give misleading results.

-- COUNT(*) counts all rows including NULLs
-- COUNT(column) skips NULLs
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
  COUNT(email) AS rows_with_email
FROM customers

One-Line Summary: NULL means missing data in SQL — use ISNULL, COALESCE, and NULLIF to handle it gracefully and avoid errors or misleading results in your queries.

blogs.title: Numeric Functions in SQL Server
blogs.description: Explore SQL Server's mathematical and numeric functions — ROUND, FLOOR, CEILING, ABS, POWER, SQRT, and more — for precise numerical calculations.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
Why Numeric Functions?

Data analysis often requires mathematical transformations — rounding prices, computing percentages, calculating distances, or applying statistical formulas. SQL Server provides a rich set of numeric functions that work directly in your queries.

ROUND()

Rounds a number to a specified number of decimal places.

SELECT ROUND(15.6789, 2)   -- 15.68
SELECT ROUND(15.6789, 0)   -- 16.0
SELECT ROUND(15.6789, -1)  -- 20.0 (round to tens)

SELECT ROUND(price, 2) AS rounded_price FROM products

FLOOR() and CEILING()

FLOOR() rounds down to the nearest integer. CEILING() rounds up.

SELECT FLOOR(4.9)    -- 4
SELECT CEILING(4.1)  -- 5
SELECT FLOOR(-4.1)   -- -5
SELECT CEILING(-4.9) -- -4

ABS()

Returns the absolute (positive) value of a number.

SELECT ABS(-150)    -- 150
SELECT ABS(150)     -- 150

-- Calculate variance from target
SELECT ABS(actual - target) AS variance FROM sales_data

POWER() and SQRT()

SELECT POWER(2, 10)   -- 1024 (2 to the power of 10)
SELECT SQRT(144)      -- 12.0 (square root)

MODULO Operator (%)

Returns the remainder after division. Useful for checking even/odd or grouping into buckets.

SELECT 17 % 5     -- 2

-- Find all even-numbered IDs
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_id % 2 = 0

Arithmetic in SQL

SELECT
  price,
  quantity,
  price * quantity AS gross_revenue,
  price * quantity * 0.9 AS after_discount,
  ROUND(price * 1.18, 2) AS price_with_tax
FROM order_items

One-Line Summary: SQL Server's numeric functions — ROUND, FLOOR, CEILING, ABS, POWER, SQRT — let you perform mathematical transformations and calculations directly inside your queries.

blogs.title: Subqueries in SQL: Queries Within Queries
blogs.description: Understand how to write and use subqueries in the SELECT, FROM, and WHERE clauses — including correlated subqueries and comparisons using ANY and ALL.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is a Subquery?

A subquery (also called an inner query or nested query) is a SQL query written inside another query. The outer query uses the result of the inner query. Subqueries are a powerful way to break complex problems into smaller, logical steps.

Subquery in WHERE Clause

The most common use — filter rows based on the result of another query.

-- Find customers who have placed at least one order
SELECT name FROM customers
WHERE customer_id IN (
  SELECT customer_id FROM orders
)

-- Find products more expensive than the average price
SELECT name, price FROM products
WHERE price > (
  SELECT AVG(price) FROM products
)

WHERE IN Subquery

SELECT * FROM employees
WHERE department_id IN (
  SELECT department_id FROM departments WHERE location = 'New York'
)

WHERE ANY and WHERE ALL

  • ANY — True if the condition is true for at least one value returned by the subquery
  • ALL — True if the condition is true for every value returned by the subquery
-- Products more expensive than ANY product in category 'Budget'
SELECT name FROM products
WHERE price > ANY (
  SELECT price FROM products WHERE category = 'Budget'
)

-- Products more expensive than ALL products in category 'Budget'
SELECT name FROM products
WHERE price > ALL (
  SELECT price FROM products WHERE category = 'Budget'
)

Subquery in SELECT Clause

Returns a scalar (single) value for each row in the outer query.

SELECT
  name,
  (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id) AS order_count
FROM customers c

Subquery in FROM Clause (Derived Table)

Creates a temporary result set that the outer query treats like a table.

SELECT country, avg_spend
FROM (
  SELECT country, AVG(total) AS avg_spend
  FROM orders o
  JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  GROUP BY country
) AS country_stats
WHERE avg_spend > 500

Correlated Subquery

A subquery that references a column from the outer query. It runs once per row of the outer query — useful but can be slow on large datasets.

-- Find employees who earn more than the average in their department
SELECT name, salary, department_id
FROM employees e
WHERE salary > (
  SELECT AVG(salary)
  FROM employees
  WHERE department_id = e.department_id
)

Subquery vs JOINs

  • Subqueries are often more readable and logically clear
  • JOINs are generally faster for large datasets and can return additional columns
  • Correlated subqueries can be slow — consider rewriting as a JOIN or CTE

One-Line Summary: Subqueries are queries nested inside other queries — they can appear in WHERE, SELECT, or FROM clauses and are powerful tools for solving complex filtering and calculation problems.

blogs.title: CTEs in SQL Server: Common Table Expressions Explained
blogs.description: Learn how to use Common Table Expressions (CTEs) to write cleaner, more readable SQL — including standalone CTEs, multiple CTEs, nested CTEs, and recursive CTEs.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is a CTE?

A Common Table Expression (CTE) is a named, temporary result set defined at the beginning of a query using the WITH keyword. It exists only for the duration of that query. CTEs make complex queries easier to read, test, and maintain by breaking them into logical named blocks.

Basic CTE Syntax

WITH cte_name AS (
  -- Your subquery here
  SELECT column1, column2
  FROM table_name
  WHERE condition
)
SELECT * FROM cte_name

Standalone CTE

A single CTE used to simplify one complex query.

WITH high_value_orders AS (
  SELECT customer_id, SUM(total) AS total_spent
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id
  HAVING SUM(total) > 1000
)
SELECT c.name, h.total_spent
FROM customers c
JOIN high_value_orders h ON c.customer_id = h.customer_id

Multiple CTEs

Chain multiple CTEs separated by commas to create a multi-step data pipeline in one query.

WITH
orderly_customers AS (
  SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
rich_customers AS (
  SELECT customer_id, SUM(total) AS total_spent
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT c.name, o.order_count, r.total_spent
FROM customers c
JOIN orderly_customers o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
JOIN rich_customers r ON c.customer_id = r.customer_id

Nested CTE (CTE Referencing Another CTE)

WITH
base_data AS (
  SELECT country, SUM(total) AS revenue
  FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
  GROUP BY country
),
ranked_countries AS (
  SELECT country, revenue,
    RANK() OVER (ORDER BY revenue DESC) AS rnk
  FROM base_data
)
SELECT * FROM ranked_countries WHERE rnk <= 5

Recursive CTE

A recursive CTE references itself. Perfect for hierarchical data like org charts, bill-of-materials, or category trees.

WITH org_chart AS (
  -- Anchor: start with top-level managers
  SELECT employee_id, name, manager_id, 1 AS level
  FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL

  UNION ALL

  -- Recursive: join employees to their manager
  SELECT e.employee_id, e.name, e.manager_id, o.level + 1
  FROM employees e
  INNER JOIN org_chart o ON e.manager_id = o.employee_id
)
SELECT * FROM org_chart ORDER BY level

CTE vs Subquery vs Temp Table

  • CTE — Best for readability, reuse within one query, and recursion. Not persisted to disk.
  • Subquery — Inline, less reusable, can get messy when nested deeply
  • Temp Table — Persisted to tempdb, useful when results are needed across multiple queries

One-Line Summary: CTEs let you define named temporary result sets with WITH, making complex SQL queries dramatically more readable — and recursive CTEs make hierarchical data traversal elegant.

blogs.title: Window Functions in SQL Server: Analytics Without Collapsing Rows
blogs.description: Master SQL Server window functions — OVER, PARTITION BY, ORDER BY, frames, and the difference between window functions and GROUP BY.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What Are Window Functions?

A window function performs calculations across a set of rows related to the current row — without collapsing those rows into a single output row like GROUP BY does. The word 'window' refers to the 'window' of rows considered for each calculation.

GROUP BY vs Window Functions

  • GROUP BY — Collapses rows into one row per group. You lose detail.
  • Window Functions — Compute aggregates while keeping all individual rows intact. You gain both detail and summary together.
-- GROUP BY: only one row per country
SELECT country, SUM(total) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY country

-- Window Function: keep all rows AND show total
SELECT order_id, country, total,
  SUM(total) OVER (PARTITION BY country) AS country_total
FROM orders

The OVER Clause

Every window function uses OVER() to define the window. Without any arguments, OVER() applies the function to the entire result set.

SELECT name, salary,
  AVG(salary) OVER() AS company_avg_salary
FROM employees

PARTITION BY

Divides rows into partitions (groups). The window function resets for each partition — similar to GROUP BY but without collapsing rows.

SELECT name, department, salary,
  AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department) AS dept_avg
FROM employees

ORDER BY in Window Functions

Defines the order of rows within each window for running/cumulative calculations.

SELECT order_id, order_date, total,
  SUM(total) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS running_total
FROM orders

Window Frame

The frame specifies which rows within the partition the function should consider relative to the current row.

-- Running total from the beginning to current row
SUM(total) OVER (ORDER BY order_date
  ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)

-- Moving 3-day average
AVG(daily_sales) OVER (ORDER BY sale_date
  ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)

Rules of Window Functions

  • Window functions are processed after WHERE, GROUP BY, and HAVING — but before ORDER BY and SELECT
  • They cannot appear in WHERE or GROUP BY clauses
  • Use a subquery or CTE to filter on window function results

One-Line Summary: Window functions use OVER() to perform calculations across related rows while keeping all rows visible — giving you both row-level detail and group-level analytics simultaneously.

blogs.title: Window Aggregate Functions: SUM, COUNT, AVG, MIN, MAX with OVER
blogs.description: Apply aggregate functions as window functions to compute running totals, cumulative averages, and partition-level summaries while retaining full row detail.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
Window Aggregates Overview

All standard aggregate functions — SUM(), COUNT(), AVG(), MIN(), MAX() — can be used as window functions by adding an OVER() clause. This makes them far more powerful than their GROUP BY equivalents.

SUM() as Window Function

-- Total revenue per category alongside each product
SELECT product_name, category, price,
  SUM(price) OVER (PARTITION BY category) AS category_total
FROM products

-- Running total (cumulative sum) over time
SELECT order_date, daily_sales,
  SUM(daily_sales) OVER (ORDER BY order_date
    ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS cumulative_sales
FROM daily_revenue

COUNT() as Window Function

-- How many orders each customer has, shown next to each order
SELECT customer_id, order_id, order_date,
  COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id) AS total_orders
FROM orders

AVG() as Window Function

-- Compare each employee salary to their department average
SELECT name, department, salary,
  AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department) AS dept_avg,
  salary - AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department) AS diff_from_avg
FROM employees

MIN() and MAX() as Window Functions

-- Show product price alongside the cheapest and most expensive in category
SELECT product_name, category, price,
  MIN(price) OVER (PARTITION BY category) AS cheapest,
  MAX(price) OVER (PARTITION BY category) AS most_expensive
FROM products

Cumulative Analysis Pattern

One of the most common use cases in data analytics — computing running/cumulative totals over time.

SELECT
  YEAR(order_date) AS year,
  MONTH(order_date) AS month,
  SUM(total) AS monthly_revenue,
  SUM(SUM(total)) OVER (
    PARTITION BY YEAR(order_date)
    ORDER BY MONTH(order_date)
  ) AS ytd_revenue
FROM orders
GROUP BY YEAR(order_date), MONTH(order_date)
ORDER BY year, month

One-Line Summary: Using SUM, COUNT, AVG, MIN, and MAX with OVER() enables powerful analytics like running totals, cumulative averages, and group comparisons — all while keeping every row in the result set.

blogs.title: Window Ranking Functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, NTILE
blogs.description: Master ranking window functions in SQL Server — ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, NTILE, PERCENT_RANK, and CUME_DIST — to rank, number, and segment your data.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
Why Ranking Functions?

Ranking functions assign a rank or number to each row within a window partition based on ordering. They are essential for leaderboards, identifying top-N records, paginating results, and segmenting data into quantiles.

ROW_NUMBER()

Assigns a unique sequential integer to each row. No ties — every row gets a unique number.

-- Rank employees by salary within each department
SELECT name, department, salary,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS row_num
FROM employees

-- Get the top 1 earner per department
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn
  FROM employees
) ranked WHERE rn = 1

RANK()

Assigns a rank with gaps for ties. Two rows tied at rank 1 means the next rank is 3, not 2.

SELECT name, salary,
  RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS salary_rank
FROM employees
-- Result: 1, 1, 3, 4, 4, 6...

DENSE_RANK()

Like RANK() but no gaps in ranking. Two rows tied at rank 1 means the next row is rank 2.

SELECT name, salary,
  DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dense_rank
FROM employees
-- Result: 1, 1, 2, 3, 3, 4...

NTILE(n)

Distributes rows into n roughly equal buckets. Great for quartiles, quintiles, or deciles.

-- Divide customers into 4 spending quartiles
SELECT customer_id, total_spent,
  NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY total_spent DESC) AS quartile
FROM customer_spending
-- Quartile 1 = top 25% spenders

PERCENT_RANK()

Returns the relative rank of a row as a percentage between 0 and 1.

SELECT name, salary,
  PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS pct_rank
FROM employees
-- 0 = lowest, 1 = highest

CUME_DIST()

Returns the cumulative distribution — the proportion of rows with values less than or equal to the current row's value.

SELECT name, salary,
  CUME_DIST() OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS cumulative_dist
FROM employees
-- 0.5 means this salary is higher than 50% of employees

ROW_NUMBER vs RANK vs DENSE_RANK

  • ROW_NUMBER — Always unique, no ties possible. Best for pagination and deduplication.
  • RANK — Ties share the same rank, with gaps. Good for competitive rankings.
  • DENSE_RANK — Ties share the same rank, no gaps. Good for medal-style rankings.

One-Line Summary: SQL Server ranking functions — ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, NTILE, PERCENT_RANK, CUME_DIST — assign positions and percentiles to rows within partitions, powering leaderboards, segmentation, and pagination.

blogs.title: Window Value Functions: LAG, LEAD, FIRST_VALUE & LAST_VALUE
blogs.description: Learn how to access values from other rows using SQL Server's window value functions — LAG, LEAD, FIRST_VALUE, and LAST_VALUE — for period-over-period analysis.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What Are Value Functions?

Value window functions allow you to access data from other rows in the window without a self-join. They are essential for period-over-period comparisons, trend analysis, and identifying changes over time.

LAG()

Returns the value from a previous row in the window. Perfect for month-over-month or day-over-day comparisons.

-- LAG(column, offset, default)
SELECT order_date, revenue,
  LAG(revenue, 1, 0) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_month_revenue,
  revenue - LAG(revenue, 1, 0) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS mom_change
FROM monthly_revenue

LEAD()

Returns the value from a future/following row. Useful for forecasting comparisons or looking ahead.

-- Compare current month with next month
SELECT order_date, revenue,
  LEAD(revenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS next_month_revenue
FROM monthly_revenue

FIRST_VALUE()

Returns the first value in the window frame. Useful for benchmarking against the first period or the baseline.

-- Show revenue vs the first month of the year
SELECT order_month, revenue,
  FIRST_VALUE(revenue) OVER (
    PARTITION BY YEAR(order_month)
    ORDER BY order_month
  ) AS jan_baseline
FROM monthly_revenue

LAST_VALUE()

Returns the last value in the window frame. Note: requires explicit frame definition to work correctly with the full partition.

SELECT order_month, revenue,
  LAST_VALUE(revenue) OVER (
    PARTITION BY YEAR(order_month)
    ORDER BY order_month
    ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
  ) AS year_end_revenue
FROM monthly_revenue

Practical Use Case: YoY Growth

WITH monthly AS (
  SELECT YEAR(order_date) AS yr, MONTH(order_date) AS mo,
    SUM(total) AS revenue
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY YEAR(order_date), MONTH(order_date)
)
SELECT yr, mo, revenue,
  LAG(revenue, 12) OVER (ORDER BY yr, mo) AS same_month_last_year,
  ROUND(100.0 * (revenue - LAG(revenue,12) OVER (ORDER BY yr, mo))
    / NULLIF(LAG(revenue,12) OVER (ORDER BY yr, mo), 0), 1) AS yoy_pct
FROM monthly

One-Line Summary: LAG, LEAD, FIRST_VALUE, and LAST_VALUE let you reference values from other rows within a window — enabling period-over-period comparisons and trend analysis without complex self-joins.

blogs.title: SQL Views: Creating Virtual Tables for Reusability and Security
blogs.description: Learn what SQL views are, why they matter, and how to use them to hide complexity, enforce security, create virtual data marts, and support multiple consumers.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is a View?

A view is a saved SQL query stored in the database with a name. It behaves like a virtual table — you can SELECT from it just like a real table — but it doesn't store data itself. Every time you query a view, the underlying SQL query runs.

-- Create a view
CREATE VIEW vw_active_customers AS
SELECT customer_id, name, email, country
FROM customers
WHERE status = 'Active'

-- Query the view like a table
SELECT * FROM vw_active_customers WHERE country = 'Germany'

Why Use Views?

1. Hide Complexity

Views abstract away complex multi-table joins and business logic. Non-technical users and BI tools can query a simple view without understanding the underlying schema.

CREATE VIEW vw_order_summary AS
SELECT c.name, o.order_date, o.total, p.product_name
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id

2. Security

Views can expose only specific columns or rows to users, hiding sensitive data like salaries or personal identifiers.

-- Expose only non-sensitive employee data
CREATE VIEW vw_employee_public AS
SELECT employee_id, name, department, job_title
FROM employees
-- salary, SSN, etc. are not exposed

3. Flexibility and Dynamic Data

Unlike a snapshot, views always reflect the latest data from the underlying tables.

4. Virtual Data Marts

In data warehousing, views can serve as lightweight virtual data marts — pre-joining dimension and fact tables for specific business domains (Sales, Finance, HR) without duplicating data.

5. Support Multiple Languages / Tools

Different teams (Python, R, Power BI) can all consume the same view without each writing their own complex query.

Modifying and Dropping Views

-- Modify a view
ALTER VIEW vw_active_customers AS
SELECT customer_id, name, email FROM customers WHERE status = 'Active'

-- Drop a view
DROP VIEW vw_active_customers

One-Line Summary: Views are saved queries that act as virtual tables — they hide complexity, enforce security, enable reusability, and power virtual data marts without duplicating data.

blogs.title: Temporary Tables in SQL Server: #TempTables and ##GlobalTempTables
blogs.description: Understand local and global temporary tables in SQL Server, when to use them over CTEs and subqueries, and how they improve performance in multi-step workflows.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What Are Temporary Tables?

Temporary tables are tables that exist only temporarily — they are stored in SQL Server's tempdb database and are automatically dropped when no longer needed. They are useful for storing intermediate results in complex, multi-step queries or stored procedures.

Local Temporary Tables (#)

A local temp table (prefixed with #) is visible only to the current session and is automatically dropped when the session ends or when you explicitly drop it.

-- Create a local temp table
SELECT customer_id, SUM(total) AS total_spent
INTO #customer_spend
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id

-- Use it in a later query
SELECT c.name, cs.total_spent
FROM customers c
JOIN #customer_spend cs ON c.customer_id = cs.customer_id
WHERE cs.total_spent > 500

-- Explicitly drop when done
DROP TABLE #customer_spend

Global Temporary Tables (##)

Global temp tables (prefixed with ##) are visible to all sessions. They are dropped when the session that created them ends and no other session is referencing them.

CREATE TABLE ##shared_data (
