  id INT,
  value NVARCHAR(100)
)

Creating with CREATE vs SELECT INTO

-- Using CREATE TABLE explicitly
CREATE TABLE #results (
  product_id INT,
  product_name NVARCHAR(100),
  revenue DECIMAL(10,2)
)
INSERT INTO #results
SELECT product_id, product_name, SUM(total) AS revenue
FROM order_items GROUP BY product_id, product_name

-- Using SELECT INTO (faster, auto-creates structure)
SELECT product_id, SUM(total) AS revenue
INTO #product_revenue
FROM order_items
GROUP BY product_id

Temp Table vs CTE vs Subquery

  • Subquery — Inline, not reusable, re-executed each time
  • CTE — Named, readable, but not persisted; limited to one query block
  • Temp Table — Persisted in tempdb, reusable across multiple queries in the same session, can be indexed for better performance

When to Use Temp Tables

  • Intermediate results needed multiple times in complex procedures
  • When you need to add indexes to improve downstream query performance
  • Breaking massive queries into manageable, debuggable steps
  • Inside stored procedures for step-by-step ETL logic

One-Line Summary: Temporary tables store intermediate results in tempdb for the duration of a session — they offer more flexibility than CTEs when results need to be reused, indexed, or shared across multiple query steps.

blogs.title: Stored Procedures in SQL Server: Reusable, Parameterized SQL Programs
blogs.description: Learn how to create and use stored procedures in SQL Server — including parameters, flow control (IF/ELSE, WHILE), error handling (TRY/CATCH), and when to prefer them over Python.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is a Stored Procedure?

A stored procedure is a precompiled, saved collection of SQL statements stored in the database with a name. You can call it like a function — with or without parameters — and it executes all the SQL inside it. Stored procedures are a fundamental building block for automation, ETL pipelines, and business logic in SQL Server.

Basic Syntax

CREATE PROCEDURE usp_GetCustomersByCountry
  @country NVARCHAR(50)
AS
BEGIN
  SELECT customer_id, name, email
  FROM customers
  WHERE country = @country
END

-- Execute the procedure
EXEC usp_GetCustomersByCountry @country = 'Germany'

Input and Output Parameters

CREATE PROCEDURE usp_GetOrderCount
  @customer_id INT,
  @order_count INT OUTPUT
AS
BEGIN
  SELECT @order_count = COUNT(*)
  FROM orders
  WHERE customer_id = @customer_id
END

-- Call with output parameter
DECLARE @cnt INT
EXEC usp_GetOrderCount @customer_id = 1, @order_count = @cnt OUTPUT
SELECT @cnt AS order_count

Flow Control

Stored procedures support full programmatic logic with IF/ELSE and WHILE loops.

-- IF / ELSE
IF @total > 1000
  PRINT 'High value customer'
ELSE
  PRINT 'Regular customer'

-- WHILE loop
DECLARE @i INT = 1
WHILE @i <= 10
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO numbers VALUES (@i)
  SET @i = @i + 1
END

Error Handling with TRY/CATCH

CREATE PROCEDURE usp_TransferFunds
  @from_id INT,
  @to_id INT,
  @amount DECIMAL(10,2)
AS
BEGIN
  BEGIN TRY
    BEGIN TRANSACTION
      UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - @amount WHERE id = @from_id
      UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + @amount WHERE id = @to_id
    COMMIT TRANSACTION
  END TRY
  BEGIN CATCH
    ROLLBACK TRANSACTION
    PRINT ERROR_MESSAGE()
  END CATCH
END

Stored Procedure vs Python

  • Stored Procedures — Live inside the database, fast for data-heavy operations, no external dependencies, great for ETL and automation within SQL Server
  • Python — Better for complex algorithms, ML, APIs, file I/O, and orchestration logic outside the database
  • Best practice: use stored procedures for data transformation and Python for orchestration and ML

One-Line Summary: Stored procedures are precompiled SQL programs stored in the database — they encapsulate business logic, accept parameters, support flow control and error handling, and are essential for ETL automation in SQL Server.

blogs.title: SET Operators in SQL: UNION, UNION ALL, INTERSECT & EXCEPT
blogs.description: Learn how to combine or compare results from multiple SELECT statements using UNION, UNION ALL, INTERSECT, and EXCEPT in SQL Server.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What Are SET Operators?

SET operators combine the results of two or more SELECT statements into a single result set. For SET operators to work, both SELECT statements must have the same number of columns and compatible data types.

UNION

Combines two result sets and removes duplicates.

-- All customers from Germany or Italy (no duplicates)
SELECT customer_id, name FROM customers WHERE country = 'Germany'
UNION
SELECT customer_id, name FROM customers WHERE country = 'Italy'

-- Combine current and archive orders
SELECT order_id, total FROM orders
UNION
SELECT order_id, total FROM orders_archive

UNION ALL

Combines result sets and keeps all duplicates. Faster than UNION since it skips deduplication.

SELECT order_id, total FROM orders_2023
UNION ALL
SELECT order_id, total FROM orders_2024
-- Returns all rows from both, including duplicates

UNION vs UNION ALL

  • UNION — Removes duplicates. Use when duplicates are not acceptable. Slower.
  • UNION ALL — Keeps duplicates. Use when you want all rows or know there are no duplicates. Faster.

INTERSECT

Returns only rows that appear in both result sets.

-- Customers who exist in both the EU and premium tables
SELECT customer_id FROM eu_customers
INTERSECT
SELECT customer_id FROM premium_customers

EXCEPT

Returns rows from the first result set that do not appear in the second.

-- Customers who have NOT placed an order
SELECT customer_id FROM customers
EXCEPT
SELECT customer_id FROM orders

One-Line Summary: SET operators (UNION, UNION ALL, INTERSECT, EXCEPT) combine or compare the outputs of multiple SELECT queries — essential for merging datasets, finding overlaps, and identifying differences.

blogs.title: CASE Statement in SQL: Conditional Logic in Your Queries
blogs.description: Master the SQL CASE statement — the SQL equivalent of if/else logic — to categorize data, create calculated columns, and build dynamic query logic.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is CASE?

The CASE statement provides conditional logic directly in SQL — similar to an IF/ELSE in programming. It evaluates conditions and returns different values based on which condition is true. It can appear in SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY, and GROUP BY clauses.

Searched CASE Expression

The most flexible form — each WHEN clause has its own condition.

SELECT name, salary,
  CASE
    WHEN salary < 30000 THEN 'Junior'
    WHEN salary BETWEEN 30000 AND 70000 THEN 'Mid-Level'
    WHEN salary > 70000 THEN 'Senior'
    ELSE 'Unknown'
  END AS salary_band
FROM employees

Simple CASE Expression

Compares one expression to multiple values — cleaner when checking equality.

SELECT name,
  CASE country
    WHEN 'DE' THEN 'Germany'
    WHEN 'IT' THEN 'Italy'
    WHEN 'FR' THEN 'France'
    ELSE 'Other'
  END AS country_name
FROM customers

CASE in Aggregations

Use CASE inside aggregate functions for conditional counting or summing — a very powerful pattern.

SELECT
  COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'Active' THEN 1 END) AS active_customers,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'Inactive' THEN 1 END) AS inactive_customers,
  SUM(CASE WHEN country = 'Germany' THEN total ELSE 0 END) AS german_revenue
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id

CASE in ORDER BY

-- Custom sort order: Germany first, then Italy, then rest
SELECT * FROM customers
ORDER BY
  CASE country
    WHEN 'Germany' THEN 1
    WHEN 'Italy' THEN 2
    ELSE 3
  END

One-Line Summary: CASE is SQL's conditional expression — use it in SELECT, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, or inside aggregate functions to apply if/else logic directly within your queries.

blogs.title: SQL Server Indexes: Clustered, Non-Clustered, and Columnstore
blogs.description: Understand how SQL Server indexes work — including clustered vs. non-clustered, rowstore vs. columnstore, and unique indexes — and how to choose the right index for your use case.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is an Index?

An index is a database object that speeds up data retrieval. Without an index, SQL Server scans every row in a table to find results (full table scan). With an index, it can jump directly to the relevant rows — like an index at the back of a book.

Clustered Index

Determines the physical order in which rows are stored in the table. There can be only one clustered index per table. The primary key is automatically a clustered index by default.

CREATE CLUSTERED INDEX IX_orders_date
ON orders (order_date)

-- Tables without a clustered index are called 'heaps'

Non-Clustered Index

A separate structure that stores index keys with pointers to the actual data rows. A table can have many non-clustered indexes. Best for columns frequently used in WHERE, JOIN, or ORDER BY clauses.

CREATE NONCLUSTERED INDEX IX_customers_email
ON customers (email)

-- Multi-column (composite) non-clustered index
CREATE NONCLUSTERED INDEX IX_orders_customer_date
ON orders (customer_id, order_date)

Clustered vs Non-Clustered

  • Clustered — One per table, determines row storage order, faster for range queries
  • Non-Clustered — Multiple per table, separate structure, faster for lookup queries

Unique Index

Enforces uniqueness on a column or combination of columns. Similar to a UNIQUE constraint.

CREATE UNIQUE INDEX UX_customers_email
ON customers (email)

Rowstore vs Columnstore Index

  • Rowstore — Stores data row by row. Best for OLTP (transactional) workloads — individual lookups and updates.
  • Columnstore — Stores data column by column. Best for OLAP (analytical) workloads — aggregations over large tables.
-- Columnstore index for analytics tables
CREATE COLUMNSTORE INDEX IX_sales_cs
ON fact_sales (order_date, product_id, total)

How to Choose the Right Index

  • Add clustered index on the primary key (default behavior)
  • Add non-clustered indexes on columns used frequently in WHERE and JOIN
  • Avoid functions on indexed columns in WHERE (breaks index usage)
  • Avoid over-indexing — every index slows down INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
  • Use columnstore indexes on large fact tables in data warehouses
  • Index foreign keys to speed up JOIN operations

Index Maintenance

-- Rebuild a fragmented index
ALTER INDEX IX_orders_date ON orders REBUILD

-- Reorganize (lighter maintenance)
ALTER INDEX IX_orders_date ON orders REORGANIZE

One-Line Summary: Indexes are data structures that dramatically speed up data retrieval — choose clustered indexes for row ordering, non-clustered for lookups, and columnstore for analytical aggregations on large tables.

blogs.title: SQL Server Triggers: Automated Responses to Data Changes
blogs.description: Learn what triggers are, how to create DML triggers that fire on INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE, and practical use cases like maintaining audit logs.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is a Trigger?

A trigger is a special type of stored procedure that automatically executes in response to specific events on a table — INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE. Unlike stored procedures, triggers cannot be called directly; they fire automatically when the triggering event occurs.

Types of Triggers

  • AFTER Trigger (FOR) — Fires after the INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE operation completes. Most common type.
  • INSTEAD OF Trigger — Fires instead of the original operation. Often used on views.

Basic Trigger Syntax

CREATE TRIGGER trg_AfterInsertOrder
ON orders
AFTER INSERT
AS
BEGIN
  -- Trigger body runs here after every INSERT on orders
  PRINT 'A new order was inserted'
END

The INSERTED and DELETED Tables

Inside a trigger, SQL Server provides two special virtual tables:

  • INSERTED — Contains the new rows (available in INSERT and UPDATE triggers)
  • DELETED — Contains the old rows (available in DELETE and UPDATE triggers)
-- In an UPDATE trigger, DELETED = old values, INSERTED = new values

Maintaining Audit Logs

One of the most practical uses of triggers is automatically recording every change to sensitive tables.

-- Audit log table
CREATE TABLE employees_audit (
  audit_id INT IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
  action NVARCHAR(10),
  employee_id INT,
  old_salary DECIMAL(10,2),
  new_salary DECIMAL(10,2),
  changed_at DATETIME DEFAULT GETDATE(),
  changed_by NVARCHAR(100) DEFAULT SYSTEM_USER
)

-- Trigger to log salary changes
CREATE TRIGGER trg_AuditSalaryChange
ON employees
AFTER UPDATE
AS
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO employees_audit (action, employee_id, old_salary, new_salary)
  SELECT 'UPDATE', d.employee_id, d.salary, i.salary
  FROM DELETED d
  JOIN INSERTED i ON d.employee_id = i.employee_id
  WHERE d.salary <> i.salary
END

Drop a Trigger

DROP TRIGGER trg_AuditSalaryChange

When to Use Triggers

  • Automatic audit trails / change logs
  • Enforcing complex business rules that constraints can't handle
  • Cascading actions not covered by foreign key constraints
  • Updating derived or summary tables automatically

One-Line Summary: Triggers are automatic event-driven procedures that fire on INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE — ideal for maintaining audit logs, enforcing business rules, and keeping derived data in sync.

blogs.title: CTAS in SQL Server: Create Tables As Select for Data Engineering
blogs.description: Learn the CTAS (Create Table As Select) pattern in SQL Server — used to create physical tables from query results for snapshots, data marts, and performance optimization.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is CTAS?

CTAS (Create Table As Select) is a pattern where you create a new table and populate it with the results of a SELECT query in a single operation. It is a cornerstone of data engineering — used to build data marts, snapshots, optimized reporting tables, and staging layers.

Basic CTAS Syntax

-- SQL Server uses SELECT INTO for CTAS
SELECT *
INTO new_table
FROM source_table
WHERE condition

-- Create a table from a complex query
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.name,
  c.country,
  COUNT(o.order_id) AS total_orders,
  SUM(o.total) AS lifetime_value
INTO dim_customer_summary
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.name, c.country

CREATE TABLE + INSERT Syntax (Explicit)

-- Explicit CREATE then INSERT (gives you control over data types and constraints)
CREATE TABLE fact_daily_sales (
  sale_date DATE,
  product_id INT,
  total_revenue DECIMAL(12,2),
  units_sold INT
)

INSERT INTO fact_daily_sales
SELECT
  CAST(order_date AS DATE),
  product_id,
  SUM(total),
  COUNT(*)
FROM orders
GROUP BY CAST(order_date AS DATE), product_id

Creating Snapshots

One of the most powerful data engineering patterns — capturing the current state of data at a point in time.

-- Daily snapshot of customer status
SELECT *, GETDATE() AS snapshot_date
INTO customer_snapshot_20250801
FROM customers

-- Or using a partitioned snapshot table
INSERT INTO customer_snapshots
SELECT *, CAST(GETDATE() AS DATE) AS snapshot_date
FROM customers

Optimize Performance with CTAS

Creating pre-aggregated physical tables improves query performance dramatically for reporting.

-- Pre-aggregate monthly sales into a reporting table
SELECT
  YEAR(order_date) AS yr,
  MONTH(order_date) AS mo,
  product_id,
  SUM(total) AS revenue
INTO report_monthly_sales
FROM orders
GROUP BY YEAR(order_date), MONTH(order_date), product_id

-- Add index for fast dashboard queries
CREATE NONCLUSTERED INDEX IX_report_date_product
ON report_monthly_sales (yr, mo, product_id)

Physical Data Marts

In a data warehouse architecture, CTAS is used to create physical data marts — denormalized, pre-joined tables optimized for a specific business domain (Sales mart, Finance mart, etc.).

-- Build a physical sales data mart
SELECT
  o.order_id, o.order_date,
  c.name AS customer_name, c.country,
  p.product_name, p.category,
  oi.quantity, oi.unit_price, oi.quantity * oi.unit_price AS line_total
INTO mart_sales
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id

One-Line Summary: CTAS (Create Table As Select) is the data engineering pattern of materializing query results into physical tables — enabling snapshots, data marts, and pre-aggregated reporting tables for high-performance analytics.

blogs.title: Database Objects in SQL Server: Tables, Views, Procedures, Indexes & More
blogs.description: A comprehensive overview of all major database objects in SQL Server — understanding what each one is, when to use it, and how they fit together in a database architecture.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What Are Database Objects?

A database object is any named entity stored in a SQL Server database. Each object type serves a specific purpose in organizing, storing, transforming, or controlling data access. Understanding the full catalog of objects helps you design clean, maintainable database architectures.

Core Database Objects

  • Tables — The fundamental storage unit. Organized in rows and columns. All data in a relational database ultimately lives in tables.
  • Views — Saved SELECT queries that act as virtual tables. Hide complexity, enforce security, and provide stable interfaces for consumers.
  • Indexes — Data structures that accelerate data retrieval. Clustered, non-clustered, and columnstore variants.
  • Stored Procedures — Named, parameterized SQL programs. Encapsulate business logic and automate operations.
  • Functions (UDFs) — User-defined functions that return a scalar value or table. Used in SELECT, WHERE, and JOIN clauses.
  • Triggers — Event-driven procedures that fire automatically on INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE.
  • Sequences & Identity — Auto-increment number generators for primary keys.
  • Schemas — Logical namespaces that group objects (e.g., dbo.customers, sales.orders).
  • Constraints — Rules enforced at the table level: PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, UNIQUE, CHECK, DEFAULT.
  • Temporary Tables — Short-lived tables in tempdb for intermediate processing.

Schemas for Organization

-- Creating schemas for logical separation
CREATE SCHEMA sales
CREATE SCHEMA hr
CREATE SCHEMA finance

-- Creating objects within schemas
CREATE TABLE sales.orders (...)
CREATE TABLE hr.employees (...)
CREATE VIEW finance.vw_revenue AS ...

User-Defined Functions (UDFs)

-- Scalar function returning a single value
CREATE FUNCTION fn_GetFullName (@first NVARCHAR(50), @last NVARCHAR(50))
RETURNS NVARCHAR(100)
AS
BEGIN
  RETURN @first + ' ' + @last
END

SELECT dbo.fn_GetFullName(first_name, last_name) AS full_name FROM employees

One-Line Summary: SQL Server database objects — tables, views, indexes, stored procedures, functions, triggers, schemas, and constraints — are the building blocks of every well-designed database architecture.

blogs.title: SQL Server Performance Optimization: 30 Best Practices
blogs.description: Master SQL Server query performance with 30 actionable best practices covering indexing strategies, query writing, joins, aggregations, data types, and statistics maintenance.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
Why Performance Matters

A poorly written SQL query on a large table can take minutes or even hours. The same query, optimized, can run in seconds. SQL Server performance optimization is one of the highest-ROI skills for data engineers and analysts.

Query Writing Best Practices

  • #1 — Only SELECT what you need. Avoid SELECT * — it fetches unnecessary columns, increases memory usage, and breaks if the schema changes.
  • #2 — Avoid DISTINCT and ORDER BY unless necessary. They force SQL Server to sort data, which is expensive.
  • #3 — Use TOP to limit rows during exploration. SELECT TOP 1000 * FROM large_table prevents accidental full-table scans.
  • #5 — Avoid applying functions to indexed columns in WHERE. WHERE YEAR(order_date) = 2025 disables the index. Use WHERE order_date >= '2025-01-01' instead.
  • #6 — Avoid leading wildcards in LIKE. WHERE name LIKE '%Smith' causes a full table scan. LIKE 'Smith%' can use an index.
  • #7 — Use IN instead of multiple OR conditions. Cleaner and often faster.

JOIN Best Practices

  • #8 — Know your JOIN types. INNER JOIN is fastest for matched-only results. LEFT JOIN is slightly slower. Avoid CROSS JOINs unless intentional.
  • #9 — Always use explicit ANSI-style JOINs. Never use the old comma-separated table syntax.
  • #10 — Index JOIN columns. Ensure the ON columns in both tables are indexed.
  • #11 — Filter before joining. Reduce the dataset before joining to avoid joining millions of rows unnecessarily.
  • #12 — Aggregate before joining. Pre-aggregate large fact tables before joining to dimension tables.

Index Best Practices

  • #24 — Every table should have a clustered primary key.
  • #25 — Add non-clustered indexes on frequently queried foreign keys.
  • #26 — Avoid over-indexing. Every index adds overhead to INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE operations.
  • #27 — Regularly drop unused indexes. Use DMVs to identify indexes that are never used.
  • #30 — Use columnstore indexes on large fact tables. Combine with partitioning for best performance.

Statistics and Maintenance

  • #28 — Update table statistics weekly. The query optimizer uses statistics to choose the best execution plan. Stale statistics lead to poor plans.
  • #29 — Rebuild or reorganize fragmented indexes weekly. Fragmentation over 30% = REBUILD. Fragmentation 5-30% = REORGANIZE.
-- Update statistics
UPDATE STATISTICS orders

-- Check index fragmentation
SELECT object_name(i.object_id), i.name, ips.avg_fragmentation_in_percent
FROM sys.dm_db_index_physical_stats(DB_ID(), NULL, NULL, NULL, 'SAMPLED') ips
JOIN sys.indexes i ON ips.object_id = i.object_id AND ips.index_id = i.index_id
WHERE avg_fragmentation_in_percent > 5
ORDER BY avg_fragmentation_in_percent DESC

Data Type Best Practices

  • #21 — Avoid VARCHAR or TEXT unnecessarily. Use precise data types to save storage and improve performance.
  • #22 — Avoid VARCHAR(MAX) unless truly needed. Use VARCHAR(100) or appropriate lengths instead.

One-Line Summary: SQL Server performance optimization is about writing precise queries, indexing strategically, joining efficiently, maintaining statistics, and choosing correct data types — small improvements here compound into massive gains at scale.

blogs.title: SQL Data Analytics Project: From Raw Data to Business Insights
blogs.description: Walk through a complete data analytics project using SQL Server — from understanding the data model, writing exploratory queries, to building reports that answer real business questions.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
Project Overview

A data analytics project in SQL follows a structured workflow: understand the data → explore and profile → clean and transform → analyze → report. This project demonstrates end-to-end analytics using a sales database.

Step 1: Understand the Data Model

Before writing a single query, understand the tables and their relationships.

-- Explore available tables
SELECT TABLE_NAME FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES
WHERE TABLE_TYPE = 'BASE TABLE'

-- Understand a table's structure
SELECT COLUMN_NAME, DATA_TYPE, IS_NULLABLE
FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS
WHERE TABLE_NAME = 'orders'

Step 2: Data Profiling and Exploration

-- Row count per table
SELECT 'orders' AS tbl, COUNT(*) AS rows FROM orders
UNION ALL
SELECT 'customers', COUNT(*) FROM customers
UNION ALL
SELECT 'products', COUNT(*) FROM products

-- Check for NULLs
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total,
  COUNT(email) AS has_email,
  COUNT(*) - COUNT(email) AS missing_email
FROM customers

-- Date range of data
SELECT MIN(order_date), MAX(order_date) FROM orders

Step 3: Key Business Questions

-- Q1: Top 10 customers by revenue
SELECT TOP 10 c.name, SUM(o.total) AS revenue
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.name
ORDER BY revenue DESC

-- Q2: Monthly revenue trend
SELECT YEAR(order_date) AS yr, MONTH(order_date) AS mo,
  SUM(total) AS monthly_revenue
FROM orders
GROUP BY YEAR(order_date), MONTH(order_date)
ORDER BY yr, mo

-- Q3: Top 5 products by units sold
SELECT TOP 5 p.product_name, SUM(oi.quantity) AS units_sold
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
GROUP BY p.product_name
ORDER BY units_sold DESC

-- Q4: Country-level performance
SELECT c.country,
  COUNT(DISTINCT c.customer_id) AS customers,
  COUNT(o.order_id) AS orders,
  SUM(o.total) AS revenue
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.country
ORDER BY revenue DESC

Step 4: Advanced Analysis with Window Functions

-- Running revenue + % of total
SELECT order_date, daily_revenue,
  SUM(daily_revenue) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS cumulative_revenue,
  ROUND(100.0 * daily_revenue / SUM(daily_revenue) OVER(), 2) AS pct_of_total
FROM (
  SELECT CAST(order_date AS DATE) AS order_date, SUM(total) AS daily_revenue
  FROM orders GROUP BY CAST(order_date AS DATE)
) daily

One-Line Summary: A SQL analytics project follows a clear workflow — profile the data, ask business questions, write targeted queries, and use window functions to surface trends — turning raw tables into actionable business intelligence.

blogs.title: SQL Data Warehouse Project: Building a Star Schema in SQL Server
blogs.description: Learn how to design and build a data warehouse in SQL Server — creating dimension tables, fact tables, a star schema, and ETL pipelines to load and query analytical data.
blogs.author: SQL Notes
blogs.content:
What is a Data Warehouse?

A data warehouse is a centralized repository of integrated, historical data optimized for analytical queries and reporting. Unlike transactional databases (OLTP) which are optimized for fast individual inserts and updates, data warehouses (OLAP) are optimized for complex aggregations over large datasets.

Star Schema Design

The most common data warehouse design pattern. It consists of one central fact table surrounded by dimension tables — resembling a star shape.

  • Fact Table — Stores measurable, quantitative business events (orders, transactions, page views). Contains foreign keys to dimensions and numeric measures.
  • Dimension Tables — Store descriptive context about the facts (customers, products, dates, locations). Typically small, slowly changing.

Building Dimension Tables

-- Date dimension
CREATE TABLE dim_date (
  date_key INT PRIMARY KEY,
  full_date DATE,
  year INT,
  quarter INT,
  month INT,
  month_name NVARCHAR(20),
  week INT,
  day_of_week NVARCHAR(10),
  is_weekend BIT
)

-- Customer dimension
CREATE TABLE dim_customer (
  customer_key INT IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
  customer_id INT,
  name NVARCHAR(100),
  country NVARCHAR(50),
  segment NVARCHAR(50),
  start_date DATE,
  end_date DATE,
  is_current BIT
)

-- Product dimension
CREATE TABLE dim_product (
  product_key INT IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
  product_id INT,
  product_name NVARCHAR(100),
  category NVARCHAR(50),
  brand NVARCHAR(50)
)

Building the Fact Table

CREATE TABLE fact_sales (
  sale_key INT IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
  date_key INT FOREIGN KEY REFERENCES dim_date(date_key),
  customer_key INT FOREIGN KEY REFERENCES dim_customer(customer_key),
  product_key INT FOREIGN KEY REFERENCES dim_product(product_key),
  quantity INT,
  unit_price DECIMAL(10,2),
  total_revenue DECIMAL(10,2),
  discount DECIMAL(10,2)
)

ETL Pipeline with SQL

-- Extract: Pull from source OLTP tables
-- Transform: Clean, join, compute keys
-- Load: Insert into warehouse tables

INSERT INTO fact_sales (date_key, customer_key, product_key, quantity, unit_price, total_revenue)
SELECT
  CONVERT(INT, REPLACE(CAST(o.order_date AS DATE), '-', '')) AS date_key,
  dc.customer_key,
  dp.product_key,
  oi.quantity,
  oi.unit_price,
  oi.quantity * oi.unit_price AS total_revenue
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN dim_customer dc ON o.customer_id = dc.customer_id AND dc.is_current = 1
JOIN dim_product dp ON oi.product_id = dp.product_id

Analytical Queries on the Warehouse

-- Revenue by country and product category
SELECT dc.country, dp.category, dd.year,
  SUM(fs.total_revenue) AS revenue
FROM fact_sales fs
JOIN dim_customer dc ON fs.customer_key = dc.customer_key
JOIN dim_product dp ON fs.product_key = dp.product_key
JOIN dim_date dd ON fs.date_key = dd.date_key
GROUP BY dc.country, dp.category, dd.year
ORDER BY dd.year, revenue DESC

One-Line Summary: A SQL data warehouse uses a star schema of fact and dimension tables to organize historical business data for fast analytical queries — and ETL pipelines load, clean, and transform data from source systems into this optimized structure.
